description: Žižek at his most accessible and provocative, reading Hitchcock through Lacan, dismantling liberal ideology, and arguing that what we call "reality" is structured by fantasies we refuse to examine.

title: Issy Wood: Queen
author: Issy Wood
tagline: Uncanny, airbrushed paintings of inherited objects, food, and bodily unease by one of the most talked-about young painters.
description: Wood's paintings — rendered in an eerie, smooth technique that recalls commercial illustration — depict fur coats, meringues, leather car interiors, and fleshy close-ups with an unsettling intimacy. This catalogue presents her recent work, which circles around inheritance, luxury, disgust, and the female body.

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Wood's paintings treat objects and bodies with the same uncanny smoothness — fur coats and skin become interchangeable surfaces, exposing the uncomfortable proximity of luxury and flesh.

title: Ithell Colquhoun
author: Amy Sherlock & Laura Smith
tagline: The occultist, surrealist, and painter of enchanted Cornish landscapes, finally given her Tate retrospective.
description: Colquhoun was a painter, writer, and practising occultist who combined Surrealist technique with genuine esoteric knowledge. This Tate catalogue reveals an artist who was at once a serious participant in the British Surrealist movement and an initiate of the Golden Dawn and OTO. Her paintings of the Cornish landscape pulse with animistic energy — rocks, water, and vegetation alive with hidden forces.

themeNotes.Consciousness and the Sacred: Colquhoun's art and life were inseparable from her occult practice — painting as a form of scrying, landscape as a site of magical encounter.
themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Her paintings reveal the hidden forces she believed animated the natural world — telluric currents, elemental spirits, the living intelligence of stone and water.

title: Jean Baudrillard: Art and Artefice
author: Nicholas Zurbrugg
tagline: Zurbrugg interviews Baudrillard on art, simulation, and the fate of reality in the age of the hyperreal.
description: Extended conversations in which Baudrillard discusses his relationship to contemporary art, the difference between simulation and representation, and why he believes reality has been replaced by its own image.

title: Jeff Koons
author: Jeff Koons
tagline: The balloon dogs, vacuum cleaners, and stainless-steel rabbits that made kitsch the dominant mode of contemporary art.
description: This monograph covers Koons's career from the early readymade vacuum cleaners and basketballs to the Banality series, the Made in Heaven works, and the Celebration sculptures. Whether you regard him as a Duchampian genius or the ultimate symptom of art-market excess, the book presents the full scope of a practice that has reshaped the relationship between art, commerce, and taste.

title: Jerusalem
author: Yotam Ottolenghi & Sami Tamimi
tagline: A celebration of the food of Jerusalem, shaped by the cultures that converge there.
description: Two chefs — one Israeli, one Palestinian — both born in Jerusalem in the same year, collaborate on a book about the food of their shared, divided city. Jerusalem explores the culinary traditions of the Arab, Jewish, Christian, and Armenian communities, from the hummus stalls of the Old City to the spice markets and home kitchens. The recipes — roasted aubergine with tahini and pomegranate, lamb shawarma, chopped salads, stuffed vegetables — are deeply rooted in the city's layered history. It's a book about what food can hold when politics cannot.

title: John Baldessari: Pure Beauty
author: Jessica Morgan & Leslie Jones
tagline: The conceptualist who covered faces with coloured dots and proved that ideas could be funnier than objects.
description: Baldessari's career — from burning his own paintings to the text canvases, the movie stills with obscured faces, and the witty photographic juxtapositions — is one of the most sustained investigations into the relationship between language, image, and meaning in contemporary art. This Tate catalogue surveys his full range with characteristic playfulness.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Baldessari's obscured faces and rearranged images expose how meaning is constructed through framing, selection, and the power of captioning.

title: John Stezaker
author: John Stezaker
tagline: Vintage photographs sliced and recombined — the uncanny beauty of the interrupted image.
description: Stezaker works with found photographs — old film stills, postcards, portraits — which he cuts, overlaps, and recombines with surgical precision. A landscape postcard inserted into a Hollywood headshot produces a face that is simultaneously ruined and transfigured. This Whitechapel/Ridinghouse monograph presents his major series: Masks, Marriages, and Tabula Rasa.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Stezaker's cuts reveal what lies behind the smooth surface of the photographic image — landscape erupting through a portrait, the uncanny emerging from the banal.

title: Jonas Mekas
author: Jonas Mekas
tagline: The godfather of American avant-garde cinema — diary films, displaced memory, and the beauty of the everyday.
description: This catalogue surveys the work of the Lithuanian-born filmmaker, poet, and arts organiser who almost single-handedly built the infrastructure for American experimental cinema. Mekas's diary films — shimmering, fragmented records of daily life in New York — are among the most beautiful and humane works in the history of the moving image.

themeNotes.Place and Belonging: Mekas's diary films are the work of a displaced person — a Lithuanian exile in New York whose camera records the attempt to make a home in a foreign city.

title: Juergen Teller: Marc Jacobs Advertising 1998-2009
author: Juergen Teller
tagline: A decade of fashion advertising shot with the deliberate artlessness that became an era's defining aesthetic.
description: Teller's campaigns for Marc Jacobs — featuring Victoria Beckham in shopping bags, Sofia Coppola in a bath, and Winona Ryder post-shoplifting arrest — redefined fashion photography. Shot with on-camera flash, unflattering angles, and a studied casualness, these images blur the line between editorial, advertising, and art. Published by Steidl with Teller's characteristic refusal of glamour.

title: King Kong Theory
author: Virginie Despentes
tagline: Despentes writes about rape, prostitution, and pornography with the fury of someone who has lived through all three.
description: Part memoir, part manifesto, Despentes refuses every polite feminist position and writes from the gutter — about being raped, about working in pornography, about the anger that respectable feminism would rather not acknowledge. Raw, confrontational, and essential.

title: Kyoto: A Cultural and Culinary Guide
author: Various
tagline: An exploration of Kyoto through its food, temples, and traditions.
description: A guide to Kyoto that goes beyond the tourist trail, exploring the ancient capital through its food culture, tea ceremonies, temple gardens, and seasonal rituals. The book traces the development of kaiseki cuisine — the multi-course meal that elevates simplicity to high art — and visits the tofu shops, pickle makers, and market stalls that have sustained the city for centuries. It's a meditation on how a place can preserve its traditions while remaining alive, and on the Japanese aesthetic of finding beauty in impermanence.

title: Labyrinths
author: Jorge Luis Borges
tagline: The essential Borges — stories and essays that turn reality into an infinite library.
description: Borges creates worlds where libraries contain every possible book, where a map becomes as large as the territory it represents, where a man dreams another man into existence. These stories — "The Library of Babel," "The Garden of Forking Paths," "Pierre Menard, Author of the Quixote" — changed what fiction could do.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Borges reveals infinite structures beneath the surface of reality — libraries that contain all possible books, gardens where time forks into parallel futures. His fiction makes the hidden architecture of the world visible.

title: Les Misérables
author: Victor Hugo
tagline: Hugo's towering epic of justice, mercy, and redemption in post-revolutionary France.
description: Jean Valjean, released after nineteen years in prison for stealing a loaf of bread, breaks his parole and remakes his life — as a factory owner, a mayor, and the guardian of the orphaned Cosette. Pursued relentlessly by the inspector Javert, who believes in the law above all else, Valjean's story becomes an epic meditation on justice, mercy, and what it means to be good in an unjust society. Hugo weaves in the Paris sewers, the Battle of Waterloo, street urchins, revolutionaries, and the 1832 June Rebellion. It's sprawling, digressive, and magnificent — a novel that contains an entire civilisation.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Valjean is branded an ex-convict and permanently excluded from society for stealing bread. Hugo shows how poverty itself is a form of othering — the poor are not citizens but threats to be managed.
themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: The novel is built around reinvention after destruction — Valjean rebuilds his life from nothing, Paris rebuilds after revolution, and Hugo argues that mercy, not law, is what allows human beings to survive their own history.

title: Letters to a Young Poet
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
tagline: Rilke's advice to a young writer — on solitude, patience, and living the questions.
description: Ten letters written between 1903 and 1908 to a young aspiring poet. Rilke counsels patience, solitude, and the willingness to live within uncertainty: "Be patient toward all that is unsolved in your heart and try to love the questions themselves."

title: Letting Go
author: David R. Hawkins
tagline: Hawkins's map of consciousness and a practical method for releasing suppressed emotions.
description: Hawkins proposes a simple technique: instead of resisting, expressing, or projecting emotions, simply let them be — observe them without judgement and allow them to pass. The book maps emotional states from shame to enlightenment on a numerical scale.

title: Linder: Works 1976-2006
author: Linder Sterling
tagline: Photomontages that splice domesticity, pornography, and consumer culture into visceral feminist collage.
description: Linder's photomontages — born in the punk era and still razor-sharp — place women's bodies into washing machines, replace heads with irons, and graft domestic appliances onto pornographic imagery. This survey covers three decades of work that remains among the most powerful visual articulations of feminist rage against the reduction of women to objects of consumption.

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Linder's collages make the female body a literal battleground between domestic labour, sexual objectification, and consumer culture.
themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Her photomontages expose the visual grammar of advertising and pornography — cutting and splicing images to reveal the violence embedded in how women are pictured.

title: Living in the End Times
author: Slavoj Žižek
tagline: Žižek reads the global crisis through denial, anger, bargaining, depression, and acceptance.
description: Structured around the Kübler-Ross stages of grief, Žižek argues that global capitalism is approaching its terminal crisis and maps our collective responses — from ideological denial to revolutionary possibility.

title: Locality, Regeneration & Diversities
author: Bennett & Butler
tagline: Essays on how communities regenerate through diversity — the politics of place-making.
description: A collection examining how urban regeneration intersects with cultural diversity, community identity, and the politics of belonging in contemporary cities.

title: Londoners: The Days and Nights of London Now
author: Craig Taylor
tagline: An oral history of London told through the voices of its inhabitants.
description: Craig Taylor spent five years interviewing Londoners — over two hundred people from every corner of the city. The result is a polyphonic portrait assembled from the voices of black-cab drivers, hedge-fund managers, refugees, sex workers, lollipop ladies, and tube workers. They talk about arriving in London for the first time, about loneliness and belonging, about the smell of the city and the weight of its history. It's a book that captures the lived reality of one of the world's great cities — not the tourist London but the London of daily life, endlessly various and often profoundly moving.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: The voices in this book include refugees, immigrants, and people who feel like strangers in their own city. Taylor lets them speak for themselves, revealing how belonging in London is never settled — it's always being questioned, claimed, and contested.
themeNotes.Place and Belonging: London emerges not as a fixed place but as two hundred different cities layered on top of each other. Each voice adds another London. The book argues that a city is nothing more — or less — than the sum of the lives lived in it.

title: Looking Awry: An Introduction to Jacques Lacan through Popular Culture
author: Slavoj Žižek
tagline: Žižek reads Hitchcock, film noir, and detective fiction through Lacan — and makes psychoanalytic theory genuinely entertaining.
description: Žižek uses popular culture — Hitchcock's films, hard-boiled detective novels, science fiction — to explain Lacanian concepts like the gaze, the object petit a, and the Real. The result is both a lucid introduction to Lacan and a demonstration that popular culture is not a debased form of high theory but its natural home.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Žižek uses Lacan's theory of the gaze to show how cinema and popular culture structure our ways of looking — the act of 'looking awry' reveals what direct vision conceals.

title: Louise Bourgeois
author: Frances Morris
tagline: Spiders, cells, and the architecture of trauma — Bourgeois's ferocious, deeply personal sculptural world.
description: This Tate catalogue surveys Bourgeois's extraordinary career, from the early paintings and Personage sculptures through the Cells — room-sized installations filled with personal objects, mirrors, and bodily forms — to the monumental spider sculptures. Morris traces how Bourgeois transformed private experience — rage, jealousy, sexuality, maternal ambivalence — into some of the most powerful sculpture of the twentieth century.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Bourgeois's Cells are literal architectures of the unconscious — enclosed spaces that contain and conceal traumatic memories, childhood scenes, and hidden desires.
themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: The body in Bourgeois's work is distorted by emotion — swelling, splitting, hanging, nursing. It is always a site of psychological intensity.

title: Là-Bas
author: J.K. Huysmans
tagline: Huysmans's novel of Satanism in fin-de-siècle Paris — black masses, occultism, and the longing for the supernatural.
description: A writer researching the life of the medieval child-murderer Gilles de Rais is drawn into the world of contemporary Parisian Satanism. Huysmans writes as someone genuinely drawn to the supernatural — not debunking it but exploring its pull.

title: Malicious Damage
author: Ilsa Colsell
tagline: The story of the punk art movement and its collision with the British establishment.
description: Colsell documents the art that emerged from punk — from Jamie Reid's Sex Pistols graphics to the broader culture of DIY aesthetics, zines, and deliberate ugliness that challenged the art world's assumptions about beauty and value.

title: Martin Kippenberger: The Problem Perspective
author: Ann Goldstein
tagline: The compulsive, self-destructive, wildly prolific German artist who treated his own life as raw material.
description: Kippenberger produced paintings, sculptures, installations, posters, books, and performances at a rate that defied categorisation. This MOCA retrospective captures the range and restlessness of an artist who was simultaneously self-mythologising and self-sabotaging — a figure whose influence on subsequent generations of painters has only grown since his early death.

title: Matisse
author: Pierre Schneider
tagline: The monumental study of Matisse — colour, line, and the relentless pursuit of visual pleasure.
description: Schneider's massive monograph is widely regarded as the definitive study of Matisse's art and life. It traces the full arc from the early Fauvist paintings through the paper cut-outs, situating Matisse not as a painter of mere decoration but as an artist engaged in a profound philosophical investigation of colour, light, and sensation. A book to live with.

title: Matter and Memory
author: Henri Bergson
tagline: Bergson's radical argument that memory is not stored in the brain but persists in time itself.
description: Bergson dismantles the assumption that the brain is a container for memories, arguing instead that the past survives in its entirety and that perception is always shaped by the virtual pressure of memory. A key influence on Deleuze and on process philosophy more broadly.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: For Bergson, the entire past lies beneath every moment of perception — not stored somewhere, but coexisting with the present. Memory is what lies beneath consciousness, shaping everything we see.

title: Meeting the Universe Halfway
author: Karen Barad
tagline: Barad's "agential realism" — matter is not passive stuff but an active participant in the world's becoming.
description: Drawing on quantum physics and feminist theory, Barad argues that the boundaries between observer and observed, human and non-human, are not given but enacted through "intra-actions." A philosophically demanding rethinking of what matter, meaning, and measurement are.

title: Melancholy I–II
author: Jon Fosse
tagline: Fosse reimagines the life of the Norwegian painter Lars Hertervig — madness, art, and the borders of perception.
description: Based on the life of the nineteenth-century painter Lars Hertervig, who was committed to an asylum, Fosse writes in his characteristic hypnotic, repetitive prose — circling obsessively around moments of beauty and collapse. Art and madness become indistinguishable.

title: Mike Nelson: Extinction Beckons
author: Ralph Rugoff
tagline: Labyrinthine installations built from salvaged materials — immersive environments that smell of dust, diesel, and dread.
description: Nelson builds walk-through installations from scavenged materials: abandoned workshops, derelict pubs, makeshift mosques. The Hayward Gallery's 2023 survey gathered his major room-sized works, creating a vast, disorienting labyrinth that visitors wandered through in genuine uncertainty. His work evokes the uncanny persistence of places after their inhabitants have gone.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Nelson's installations are built from ruins — salvaged doors, broken furniture, abandoned tools — reassembled into spaces that feel haunted by vanished lives.
themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: His labyrinthine environments create a physical experience of disorientation and dread — the uncanny feeling that something is hidden just around the next corner.

title: Mind-Forg'd Manacles: William Blake and Slavery
author: Various
tagline: Essays on Blake's engagement with the abolitionist movement and his radical visual politics.
description: A collection exploring how Blake's poetry and art responded to the slave trade — his illustrations for abolitionist publications, the imagery of chains and liberation in his prophetic books, and his broader critique of empire.

title: Minima Moralia
author: Theodor W. Adorno
tagline: Reflections from a damaged life — aphorisms on culture, morality, and exile written during World War II.
description: Written in American exile during the war, these 153 aphorisms dissect the corruptions of everyday life under capitalism — from the decline of gift-giving to the impossibility of dwelling. "There is no right life in the wrong one" is its most famous line.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Written from the ruins of European civilisation, Minima Moralia asks whether any form of good life is possible in a damaged world. Adorno's answer is bleak but his persistence in asking is itself a form of intellectual resilience.

title: Modern Quilting
author: Various
tagline: Contemporary approaches to the quilt — geometric, improvisational, and liberated from tradition.
description: A practical reference for modern quilting techniques, covering design principles, fabric selection, and construction methods for quilts that draw on contemporary art and design rather than traditional patterns.

title: Morpho: Skeleton and Bone Reference Points
author: Michel Lauricella
tagline: An artist's reference guide to the human skeleton — bones as the architecture of the body.
description: Lauricella provides detailed drawings of every bone in the human body from multiple angles, with annotations showing how skeletal structure determines the surface forms that artists draw and sculpt.

title: Mothers: An Essay on Love and Cruelty
author: Jacqueline Rose
tagline: Rose argues that motherhood is the place where society deposits its most impossible demands.
description: Rose examines how mothers are simultaneously idealised and blamed — made responsible for everything that goes wrong while denied the resources to do anything right. She draws on psychoanalysis, literature, and current affairs to argue that our hatred of mothers reveals something fundamental about how societies manage their own cruelty.

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Rose shows how the mother's body becomes a site of social control — expected to sacrifice, nurture, and absorb blame. Motherhood is the battleground where society's cruelest contradictions are played out.
themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Mothers are simultaneously central to society and othered by it — idealised in the abstract, punished in practice. Rose reveals the politics behind this paradox.

title: Mythologies
author: Roland Barthes
tagline: Barthes decodes the hidden ideologies in everyday French culture — from wrestling to steak-frites to soap powder.
description: In short, brilliant essays, Barthes shows how bourgeois ideology naturalises itself through the mythologies of everyday life. A new car, a plate of food, a photograph of a Black soldier saluting the French flag — each conceals a political message beneath its apparent innocence.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Barthes reveals how power operates through the everyday — through images, objects, and cultural habits that appear natural but are in fact ideological constructions. Seeing through myth is the first act of resistance.

title: Nan Goldin: Eden and After
author: Nan Goldin
tagline: The bruised intimacy of Goldin's photography — love, addiction, and the fragile bonds between people.
description: This Phaidon survey presents Goldin's major work: the Ballad of Sexual Dependency slideshows, the portraits of friends and lovers, the self-portraits after beatings, and the later landscapes and still lifes. Goldin's photographs are acts of love and witness — a record of communities held together by affection and ravaged by AIDS, addiction, and loss.

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Goldin's photographs of bruised faces, emaciated bodies, and intimate moments make the body a document of lived experience — marked by violence, desire, illness, and tenderness.
themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Her work records communities destroyed by AIDS and addiction — but also the fierce love and beauty that persisted within them.

title: Needleworker's Dictionary
author: Pamela Clabburn
tagline: A comprehensive dictionary of needlework terms, techniques, and traditions from around the world.
description: Clabburn's dictionary covers every form of needlework — embroidery, lace, knitting, crochet, tapestry — with clear definitions, historical context, and illustrations. A reference work that treats needlework as a serious craft tradition worthy of scholarly attention.

title: Neighbors/Nachbarn
author: Collier Schorr
tagline: A photographer's decade-long entanglement with a small German town, blurring portraiture, history, and desire.
description: Schorr spent years photographing the young men of a Swabian village, dressing them in military uniforms and athletic gear, layering personal desire over collective memory. The result is a complex meditation on masculinity, national identity, and the photographer's power over her subjects.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Schorr's photographs make explicit the power dynamic between photographer and subject — desire, projection, and the construction of masculinity through the lens.
themeNotes.Place and Belonging: The project is rooted in a specific place — a German village — and asks how landscape, history, and identity are entangled.

title: Nervous States
author: William Davies
tagline: Davies on how feelings replaced facts — the rise of emotional politics in the age of Brexit and Trump.
description: Davies traces how the authority of experts was undermined by a politics of feeling — where real-time sentiment, gut instinct, and tribal loyalty replace evidence, argument, and shared reality. A diagnosis of the post-truth condition.

title: New Wave Clay: Ceramic Design, Art and Architecture
author: Tom Morris
tagline: The contemporary ceramics revival — from art pottery to architectural clay, craft as conceptual practice.
description: This survey documents the explosion of interest in ceramics among contemporary artists, designers, and architects. From studio pottery to large-scale architectural installations, the book maps a field in which the boundaries between art, craft, and design have become productively blurred.

title: Nihil Unbound
author: Ray Brassier
tagline: Brassier argues that philosophy must confront the reality of extinction — the universe's fundamental indifference to meaning.
description: A work of speculative realism that takes nihilism seriously: if the universe will end, if consciousness is an accident, and if meaning has no cosmic ground, what remains for thought? Brassier finds surprising resources in this confrontation with the void.

title: No Bad Parts
author: Richard C. Schwartz
tagline: Schwartz's Internal Family Systems model — every part of you has a positive intention, even the destructive ones.
description: The creator of Internal Family Systems therapy explains how the mind is naturally multiple — composed of parts that carry different roles (managers, firefighters, exiles). Healing comes not from suppressing difficult parts but from understanding what they protect.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Schwartz's model is built on the idea that beneath our protective parts — the anxiety, the anger, the numbing — lie exiled parts carrying pain and vulnerability. Healing means going beneath the surface to meet what was banished.

title: Non-Places: An Introduction to Supermodernity
author: Marc Augé
tagline: Augé coins the concept of "non-places" — airports, motorways, supermarkets — spaces of transit with no identity.
description: Augé argues that supermodernity produces spaces defined by their transience — places people pass through but never inhabit. The non-place is the opposite of the anthropological place: it has no history, no identity, no relation.

themeNotes.Place and Belonging: Augé defines the non-place as a space where belonging is impossible — the airport lounge, the motorway, the chain hotel. These are the spaces of modern transit that produce solitude rather than community.

title: Not Working: Why We Have to Stop
author: Josh Cohen
tagline: Cohen makes the case for creative idleness — against the cult of productivity.
description: Drawing on psychoanalysis, literature, and art, Cohen argues that the compulsion to be always working, always productive, is itself a form of pathology. Through portraits of great loafers — Andy Warhol, Emily Dickinson, Orson Welles — he recovers the value of doing nothing.

title: Nothing to Envy: Real Lives in North Korea
author: Barbara Demick
tagline: Six ordinary North Koreans tell their stories of love, survival, and escape.
description: Barbara Demick, the Los Angeles Times bureau chief in Seoul, spent six years interviewing North Korean defectors. From their testimonies she reconstructs the daily reality of life in Chongjin, North Korea's third-largest city, during the famine of the 1990s that killed perhaps a million people. We follow a doctor who watches her patients die of starvation, a couple who fall in love in a country where holding hands can mean imprisonment, a loyal party member whose faith slowly cracks, and a street orphan who survives by his wits. The title comes from a children's song: "We have nothing to envy in the world." The gap between the propaganda and the reality is the subject of this devastating book.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: North Korea's songbun system assigns citizens a political classification at birth that determines their entire life. Demick shows othering as total state control — where even your loyalty is predetermined, and stepping outside your assigned identity means death.
themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: A country collapses in slow motion — the famine, the crumbling infrastructure, the erosion of belief. Yet people fall in love, raise children, and find ways to survive. The defectors' stories are acts of resilience against a system designed to make individual survival impossible.

title: Nothing to Fear: Demystifying Death
author: Julie McFadden
tagline: A hospice nurse explains what actually happens when we die — and why it's not what you think.
description: Drawing on her experience caring for the dying, McFadden describes the physical process of death with clarity and tenderness, arguing that understanding what happens removes the fear and allows for a more peaceful ending.

title: On Mysticism: The Experience of Ecstasy
author: Simon Critchley
tagline: Critchley traces the tradition of mystical experience from Meister Eckhart to the present.
description: A philosopher takes mysticism seriously — not as irrationality but as a tradition of thought that operates at the limits of language and selfhood. Critchley reads Christian mystics, Sufis, and secular ecstatics to ask what happens when the self encounters something it cannot contain.

title: On Photography
author: Susan Sontag
tagline: Sontag's brilliant essays on how images reshape our relationship to reality.
description: Sontag's six interconnected essays, first published in the New York Review of Books, examine the ways photography has transformed modern consciousness. She argues that photographs don't simply record reality — they replace it, creating a world of images that we consume in place of direct experience. She considers photography as a form of aggression, as a democratic art, and as a tool of capitalism. She analyses Diane Arbus, discusses the ethics of photographing suffering, and traces how the camera has changed tourism, war reporting, and the very idea of beauty. Dense, provocative, and beautifully written, it remains the essential philosophical text on the medium.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Sontag argues that the camera is not a neutral tool but an instrument of power — to photograph someone is to appropriate their image, to turn them into an object of consumption. Photography doesn't document reality; it produces a new reality that serves the photographer's purposes.

title: On the Production of Subjectivity: Five Diagrams of the Finite-Infinite Relation
author: Simon O'Sullivan
tagline: How art, philosophy, and practice produce new forms of selfhood — subjectivity as something made, not given.
description: O'Sullivan draws on Deleuze, Guattari, Lacan, and Badiou to argue that subjectivity is produced through encounters with art, philosophy, and collective practice. The five 'diagrams' map different ways that the finite self relates to the infinite — through myth, tragedy, aesthetics, ethics, and what he calls 'ficto-analysis.' A creative, ambitious work of theory.

themeNotes.Consciousness and the Sacred: O'Sullivan treats the production of subjectivity as a quasi-sacred process — encounters with art and philosophy that open the finite self to something beyond it.

title: One-Dimensional Man
author: Herbert Marcuse
tagline: Marcuse on how advanced industrial society creates false needs and eliminates the possibility of opposition.
description: Writing in 1964, Marcuse argues that consumer capitalism has produced a "comfortable, smooth, reasonable, democratic unfreedom" in which genuine dissent is absorbed and neutralised. The working class has been bought off with commodities; critical thought has been flattened into one dimension.

title: Onsen and Ryokan of Japan
author: Various
tagline: A guide to Japan's traditional hot springs and inns — bathing culture as a form of life.
description: An illustrated guide to the best onsen (hot spring baths) and ryokan (traditional inns) across Japan, exploring the rituals, architecture, and philosophy of a bathing culture that treats cleansing as a spiritual practice.

title: Orientalism
author: Edward Said
tagline: How the West constructed "the Orient" as its Other — essential and still indispensable.
description: Said's thesis is that "the Orient" is not a natural fact but a European invention — a system of knowledge and representation that served colonial power by defining Eastern peoples as exotic, irrational, backward, and in need of Western governance. Drawing on literature, history, and political analysis, Said traces how this discourse was produced by scholars, novelists, and administrators from Napoleon's invasion of Egypt to the present day. Orientalism transformed the fields of literary criticism, history, and postcolonial studies, and its central insight — that knowledge and power are inseparable — remains one of the most important ideas in the humanities.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: The book that gave us the vocabulary for understanding how otherness is manufactured. Said showed that "the Orient" was a European invention — a projection of everything the West needed its Other to be: exotic, irrational, available for conquest.
themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Said's devastating insight: knowledge about "the Orient" was never neutral scholarship but a tool of imperial governance. The scholars, painters, and novelists who represented the East were — knowingly or not — producing the justification for dominating it.

title: Ottolenghi Flavour
author: Yotam Ottolenghi & Ixta Belfrage
tagline: A masterclass in how to transform vegetables into extraordinary meals.
description: Flavour is built around a simple idea: that great vegetable cooking comes from understanding three processes — charring, infusing, and ageing. Ottolenghi and Belfrage break down over 100 recipes to show how techniques like browning butter, blooming spices in oil, fermenting, and slow-roasting can coax maximum flavour from plants. The book is organised not by ingredient but by method, teaching you the principles behind dishes like curry-leaf popcorn, burnt aubergine with tahini, and hasselback beetroot with lime leaf butter. It's both a cookbook and a manifesto for rethinking what vegetables can do.

title: Outline
author: Rachel Cusk
tagline: A writer in Athens, defined by absence through the stories others tell her.
description: A novelist — unnamed, recently divorced — travels to Athens to teach a creative writing course. On the plane, at dinner, in class, she listens as the people she meets tell her their stories: a neighbour on the flight describes his three marriages; her students share fragments of their lives; an old friend recounts a holiday that went wrong. Gradually, through these conversations, the narrator's own story emerges — or rather, its outline, its negative space. Cusk has stripped the novel to its essentials: no plot, no physical description, almost no interiority. What remains is something strange and beautiful — a portrait of a woman defined by what she doesn't say.

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: The narrator has been so thoroughly emptied by divorce and motherhood that she exists only as an outline — a negative space defined by others' stories. Cusk shows what happens to a woman's self when the roles that defined her body (wife, mother) are stripped away.

title: Parables for the Virtual
author: Brian Massumi
tagline: Massumi on affect, movement, and the body's capacity to feel before it thinks.
description: Massumi develops a philosophy of affect — the body's preconscious responsiveness to the world — arguing that politics, art, and experience operate through intensities that precede and exceed conscious thought.

title: Pedagogy of the Oppressed
author: Paulo Freire
tagline: Freire's revolutionary argument that education is never neutral — it either liberates or domesticates.
description: Freire attacks the "banking" model of education — where teachers deposit knowledge into passive students — and proposes instead a dialogical pedagogy in which teacher and student learn together. Written from his experience of literacy campaigns in Brazil, it remains the most influential text in critical pedagogy.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Freire shows how education systems reproduce oppression by treating the oppressed as objects to be filled with the oppressor's knowledge. Liberation begins when the "other" becomes a subject who names their own world.
themeNotes.Seeing and Power: The banking model of education is a system for controlling what people see and know. Freire's dialogical method is an act of seeing together — shared inquiry rather than imposed truth.

title: Penguin by Design
author: Phil Baines
tagline: The visual history of Penguin Books — how cover design shaped a reading culture.
description: From the first three-stripe paperbacks of the 1930s through Jan Tschichold's typographic revolution to the contemporary covers, this book traces how Penguin's design made good books accessible and beautiful. Design history as cultural history.

title: Pensées
author: Blaise Pascal
tagline: Pascal's fragmentary masterwork — on faith, reason, the wager, and the infinite silence of the universe.
description: Left unfinished at Pascal's death in 1662, the Pensées are notes toward an apology for the Christian religion — but what survives is something stranger and more powerful: a record of a brilliant mind confronting the terror and beauty of existence.

title: Perfect Health
author: Deepak Chopra
tagline: Chopra integrates Ayurvedic medicine with modern science — health as a state of balanced consciousness.
description: An introduction to Ayurveda through the lens of mind-body medicine, explaining the three doshas and how understanding your constitutional type can guide diet, exercise, and daily routine toward optimal health.

title: Permanent Food
author: Maurizio Cattelan
tagline: Cattelan's art magazine project — composed entirely of pages stolen from other magazines.
description: Each issue of Permanent Food was assembled by tearing pages from existing magazines and recombining them into a new publication — an act of appropriation that questioned authorship, originality, and the nature of the art object.

title: Philosophical Devices
author: David Papineau
tagline: A toolkit of logical and mathematical concepts for philosophy students — sets, probability, and possible worlds.
description: Papineau introduces the formal tools that contemporary philosophy relies on: propositional logic, sets, probability, models, and modal logic. Clear, practical, and mercifully concise.

title: Phyllida Barlow
author: Phyllida Barlow
tagline: Monumental, precarious sculptures built from plywood, cement, and fabric — art that threatens to collapse under its own ambition.
description: Barlow's sculptures are anti-monumental monuments: towering structures of cheap materials — plywood, scrim, polystyrene, plaster — that look as if they might topple at any moment. This monograph surveys a practice that came to international prominence late in Barlow's career, celebrating the energy, scale, and sheer physical daring of her work.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Barlow's sculptures are built from the cheapest materials and look perpetually on the verge of collapse — precariousness as sculptural principle.

title: Picasso: Challenging the Past
author: Elizabeth Cowling & others
tagline: Picasso in dialogue with the Old Masters — El Greco, Velázquez, Ingres, and the weight of tradition.
description: This National Gallery exhibition catalogue examined Picasso's engagement with the art of the past, hanging his work alongside the paintings that inspired, provoked, and challenged him. The result is a revelation: Picasso's art is not a rejection of tradition but a violent, loving, competitive conversation with it.

title: Picasso: Minotaurs and Matadors
author: John Richardson
tagline: Picasso's lifelong obsession with bulls, bullfighting, and the minotaur — violence, sexuality, and myth.
description: This Gagosian publication traces the bull and minotaur through Picasso's work, from the early Spanish scenes through Guernica to the late etchings. The minotaur — half man, half beast — became Picasso's most potent alter ego, a figure through which he explored desire, cruelty, creativity, and the artist's relationship to his own mythic self-image.

title: Pleasure Activism
author: adrienne maree brown
tagline: brown argues that justice work must be rooted in pleasure, not just resistance.
description: A collection of essays, interviews, and practices exploring the idea that social justice should feel good — that pleasure is not a distraction from the work but the point of it. brown draws on Audre Lorde's "Uses of the Erotic" to build a politics grounded in joy.

title: Plenty
author: Yotam Ottolenghi
tagline: The cookbook that changed how we think about vegetables — bold, inventive, plants centre stage.
description: Before Plenty, vegetarian cooking in Britain was largely an afterthought — worthy but dull. Ottolenghi changed all that with this collection from his Guardian column, proving that vegetables could be the star of any meal. Recipes like roasted butternut squash with burnt aubergine and pomegranate, mushroom lasagne with ricotta, and saffron tagliatelle move far beyond the expected. The book draws on Middle Eastern, Mediterranean, and Asian traditions, always with Ottolenghi's signature abundance — more herbs, more spice, more colour. It launched a revolution in how we eat.

title: Poetics of Relation
author: Édouard Glissant
tagline: Glissant's lyrical philosophy of creolisation — identity not as root but as rhizome, relation as the ground of being.
description: Glissant proposes a 'poetics of relation' in which identity is not fixed but constituted through contact, exchange, and mutual transformation. Writing from the Caribbean experience of slavery, colonialism, and creolisation, he argues against monolithic identities and for a vision of the world as an interconnected, opaque, endlessly translating archipelago. One of the most beautiful works of postcolonial philosophy.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Glissant argues for the 'right to opacity' — the refusal to be made transparent to the Western gaze. Otherness is not a problem to be solved but a condition to be respected.
themeNotes.Place and Belonging: The Caribbean archipelago becomes a model for a new kind of belonging — not rooted in a single origin but branching through relation.

title: Post-Growth Living: For an Alternative Hedonism
author: Kate Soper
tagline: A philosophical case for finding pleasure in consuming less — hedonism, not austerity, as the path beyond growth.
description: Soper argues that the environmental crisis demands not puritan sacrifice but a new kind of pleasure — what she calls 'alternative hedonism.' The satisfactions of slower travel, less work, better food, and richer social life are not consolation prizes for giving up growth but genuine improvements in human experience. A rare philosophical voice that makes degrowth feel desirable.

themeNotes.The Machine and the Human: Soper challenges the equation of progress with ever-increasing production and consumption — arguing that the growth machine impoverishes the very pleasures it claims to deliver.

title: Postmodernism, or, The Cultural Logic of Late Capitalism
author: Fredric Jameson
tagline: Jameson's landmark analysis of postmodern culture as the cultural expression of multinational capitalism.
description: Jameson argues that postmodernism is not merely an aesthetic style but the cultural logic of a new stage of capitalism — characterised by the erosion of historical sense, the dominance of surface and pastiche, and the colonisation of the unconscious by the market.

title: Powers of Horror: An Essay on Abjection
author: Julia Kristeva
tagline: Kristeva's theory of abjection — the primal horror of what the body expels and the self cannot contain.
description: Kristeva argues that the abject — bodily fluids, corpses, the skin on warm milk — provokes a visceral revulsion that precedes and founds the distinction between self and other, clean and unclean, inside and outside. Drawing on psychoanalysis, anthropology, and a brilliant reading of Céline, she traces how culture manages the horror of the body's permeability.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Abjection is what lies beneath civilised subjectivity — the horror of the body's fluids, orifices, and mortality that culture works ceaselessly to repress.
themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Kristeva's abject body is the site where the distinction between self and other, inside and outside, is violently negotiated.

title: Practicing Dying
author: Charlotte Northedge
tagline: A meditation on mortality and the art of letting go — dying as a practice, not an event.
description: Northedge explores how contemplating death — practising dying, as Plato advised — can transform how we live. Drawing on philosophy, memoir, and spiritual tradition, she makes the case that death-awareness is a form of freedom.

title: Prince of Networks
author: Graham Harman
tagline: Harman's study of Bruno Latour — and the development of object-oriented ontology from actor-network theory.
description: Harman reads Latour as a metaphysician, showing how actor-network theory implies a radical ontology in which objects of every kind — human and non-human — are equally real actors. A key text in the speculative realism movement.

title: Rachel Whiteread: House
author: James Lingwood
tagline: The concrete cast of a terraced house in Bow — monument, ruin, and ghost of working-class London.
description: Whiteread's House — a complete concrete cast of the interior of a demolished Victorian terrace — was one of the most extraordinary and controversial artworks of the 1990s. This Phaidon book documents the work's creation, brief existence, and politically charged demolition, alongside critical essays that explore its resonance as a memorial to vanished domestic life.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: House was a monument to demolition itself — a cast of domestic space that became a ruin the moment the walls around it were removed.
themeNotes.Place and Belonging: The work memorialised a specific East London street and the working-class community that had lived there — destroyed by property development.

title: Radical Happiness: Moments of Collective Joy
author: Lynne Segal
tagline: Happiness as a political problem — why individual well-being can never substitute for collective joy.
description: Segal argues against the privatisation of happiness, insisting that genuine flourishing is collective, not individual. Drawing on feminism, psychoanalysis, and political theory, she traces how the happiness industry pathologises discontent while the conditions for shared joy — community, solidarity, public space — are systematically destroyed.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Segal shows how the imperative to be individually happy functions as a form of political control — privatising discontent and pathologising those who refuse to smile.

title: Radical Pedagogies
author: Various
tagline: An atlas of experimental architectural education from the 1950s to the present.
description: Case studies of radical experiments in how architecture has been taught — from the Bauhaus to Black Mountain College to contemporary design studios that challenged the assumptions of the profession.

title: Reading Seminars I and II: Lacan's Return to Freud
author: Richard Feldstein, Bruce Fink & Maire Jaanus
tagline: A scholarly guide to Lacan's first two seminars — the return to Freud via linguistics, cybernetics, and the Purloined Letter.
description: This collection of essays by leading Lacanian scholars provides close readings of Lacan's Seminars I (Freud's Papers on Technique) and II (The Ego in Freud's Theory). Essential companion reading for anyone working through Lacan's notoriously difficult early seminars, covering the mirror stage, the imaginary, the symbolic, and the foundations of Lacanian psychoanalysis.

title: Renegade: The Lives and Tales of Mark E. Smith
author: Mark E. Smith
tagline: The Fall frontman's autobiography — cantankerous, hilarious, and utterly uncompromising.
description: Mark E. Smith tells his own story with the same belligerent originality he brought to thirty years of leading The Fall — ranting about music, Thatcher, modern art, and the decline of the British working class.

title: Richard Avedon: In the American West
author: Richard Avedon
tagline: Stark, unflinching studio portraits of ranch hands, drifters, and oil workers — America stripped of its mythology.
description: Avedon spent five years photographing working people across the American West: slaughterhouse workers, miners, rodeo cowboys, waitresses. Shot against a plain white backdrop in merciless detail, these portraits strip away romantic notions of the frontier, revealing instead the physical toll of manual labour and the raw, unguarded faces of people rarely seen in art.

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Avedon's subjects bear the marks of their labour on their bodies — grease-stained skin, weathered faces, calloused hands. The body is a document of class.
themeNotes.Place and Belonging: The American West as a place of real work and real poverty, not cowboy myth.

title: Richard Hamilton & Dieter Roth: Collaborations
author: Richard Hamilton & Dieter Roth
tagline: Two restless artists — one British Pop, one Swiss anti-art — meet in a decade of collaborative invention.
description: Hamilton and Roth's collaborations combined Hamilton's precision with Roth's anarchic energy, producing prints, paintings, and objects that neither artist would have made alone. This catalogue documents a friendship conducted through art — playful, competitive, and mutually transformative.

title: Richard Prince
author: Richard Prince
tagline: The re-photographer who made other people's images his own — cowboys, nurses, jokes, and the slippage of authorship.
description: Prince's practice of re-photographing advertisements, collecting jokes, and appropriating images has raised questions about originality and authorship that remain unresolved. This Spector Books publication presents his major bodies of work — the Cowboys, the Nurses, the Gangs, and the joke paintings — with the enigmatic cool that defines his aesthetic.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Prince's re-photography makes explicit that all images are already framed, selected, and mediated — he simply adds another layer of appropriation to expose the power structures already present.

title: Roman Gardens: Villas of the Countryside
author: Various
tagline: An illustrated exploration of the gardens that adorned Roman villas.
description: This illustrated volume reconstructs the gardens of ancient Rome — from the grand peristyle gardens of Pompeian villas to the rural estates described by Pliny the Younger. Drawing on archaeological evidence, literary sources, and surviving frescoes, it explores how Romans used gardens as spaces for contemplation, entertainment, and the display of wealth and taste. Topics include water features, topiary, kitchen gardens, sacred groves, and the philosophical traditions that shaped Roman attitudes to nature. A fascinating window into how one of history's great civilisations lived with and shaped its landscape.

themeNotes.Place and Belonging: Roman gardens were designed landscapes — nature shaped by philosophy, politics, and aesthetics. They show how a civilisation expressed its relationship to the natural world, turning land into a mirror of its values and desires.

title: Romanticism and Art
author: William Vaughan
tagline: A World of Art survey of Romantic painting — from Caspar David Friedrich to Turner to Delacroix.
description: Vaughan traces how the Romantic movement transformed visual art — privileging emotion over reason, the sublime over the beautiful, and the individual genius over classical convention.

title: Rosalind Nashashibi
author: Rosalind Nashashibi
tagline: Slow, contemplative films and paintings that observe communities and landscapes with gentle attention.
description: Nashashibi's films and paintings share a quality of patient looking — whether observing Gazan families, haulage workers in Glasgow, or monastic communities. This ICA/Bergen Kunsthall publication presents her practice of attentive witness, in which the camera's stillness becomes a form of respect.

themeNotes.Place and Belonging: Nashashibi's films are always grounded in specific places — Gaza, Glasgow, a monastery — and observe the rhythms of daily life that make a community.

title: Sarah Lucas
author: Sarah Lucas
tagline: Tabloid wit, cigarettes, and fried eggs — the sculptor who made British vulgarity into high art.
description: Lucas's work — stuffed-stocking sculptures, concrete penises, self-portraits with fried eggs on her chest — is among the most viscerally funny and confrontational art to emerge from 1990s Britain. This Tate catalogue surveys her full career, from the YBA years to the monumental concrete sculptures that represented Britain at the Venice Biennale.

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Lucas's stuffed stockings, fried eggs, and concrete casts reduce the body to its most basic sexual signifiers — then dare you to laugh.

title: Screenprinting: The Complete Water-Based System
author: Robert Adam & Carol Robertson
tagline: A practical manual for water-based screenprinting — from coating screens to pulling the perfect print.
description: A thorough, well-illustrated guide to water-based screenprinting techniques for artists, covering equipment, materials, registration, and printing on various surfaces. The standard reference for those who prefer water-based inks to solvent-based systems.

title: Selected Poetry
author: Lord Byron
tagline: Byron's essential poems — from the romantic exuberance of Childe Harold to the satirical brilliance of Don Juan.
description: The poet who was "mad, bad, and dangerous to know" at his best: the passionate, witty, and scandalous verse that made him the most famous poet in Europe.

title: Selected Poetry
author: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe
tagline: Goethe's poetry in English translation — from the early Sturm und Drang lyrics to the late West-Eastern Divan.
description: Spanning six decades of writing, this selection reveals the range of Germany's greatest poet — erotic lyrics, philosophical ballads, nature poems, and the extraordinary late encounter with Persian poetry.

title: Sensoria: Thinkers for the Twenty-First Century
author: McKenzie Wark
tagline: Short, sharp essays on contemporary thinkers — from Haraway to Hito Steyerl — as a toolkit for the present.
description: Wark presents brief, lively engagements with twenty-first-century thinkers across philosophy, art, and media theory. Each essay is a quick, opinionated introduction designed to be useful rather than comprehensive — a pocket guide to contemporary thought for people who want to think differently about the world they live in.

title: Sex Energy: The Sexual Force in Man and Animals
author: Robert S. de Ropp
tagline: A biochemist's unorthodox exploration of sexual energy as a creative and spiritual force.
description: De Ropp — a biochemist and student of Gurdjieff — examines sexual energy not as something to be repressed or indulged but as a transformative force that can be directed toward creative and spiritual development. Written in the late 1960s, it combines scientific knowledge with esoteric tradition in a way that remains provocative.

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: De Ropp treats sexual energy as the body's most potent force — a battlefield between biological drive, cultural repression, and spiritual aspiration.
themeNotes.Consciousness and the Sacred: The book argues that sexual energy, properly understood and directed, is the raw material of spiritual transformation.

title: Shantaram
author: Gregory David Roberts
tagline: An escaped convict finds refuge in the slums of Bombay.
description: Lin, an Australian heroin addict and convicted armed robber, escapes from prison and flees to Bombay, where he disappears into the city's underworld. He lives in a slum, works as a counterfeiter, joins the Bombay mafia, fights in Afghanistan, and falls in love — all while trying to understand the nature of good and evil. Based loosely on Roberts's own life, Shantaram is a sprawling, addictive novel that reads like a mixture of Conrad, Kipling, and a crime thriller. It's messy, overwrought, and completely irresistible — the kind of book you stay up all night to finish.

themeNotes.Place and Belonging: A fugitive with no right to be anywhere finds a home in a Bombay slum. Roberts shows how belonging can be earned through presence, generosity, and the willingness to be changed by a place — even when you have no legal claim to it.

title: Shinjuku
author: Daido Moriyama
tagline: Moriyama's love letter to Shinjuku — the Tokyo district that has been his obsessive subject for decades.
description: Shinjuku is Moriyama's territory: the neon, the crowds, the back alleys, the sex shops, the rain-slicked streets. This photobook concentrates his vision on the single neighbourhood that has defined his work, producing a portrait of a place that is simultaneously documentary and hallucinatory.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Moriyama's Shinjuku is the city's unconscious — the nocturnal, marginal, erotic underside of Tokyo's respectable surface.
themeNotes.Place and Belonging: Moriyama has spent a lifetime photographing Shinjuku, creating a body of work that constitutes the deepest photographic relationship with a single place in the history of the medium.

title: Sigmund Freud (Volume VIII)
author: Sigmund Freud
tagline: Freud's later theoretical works — beyond the pleasure principle, the ego and the id.
description: The eighth volume of Freud's collected works, encompassing his later metapsychological writings — the death drive, the structural model of the psyche, and the cultural criticism of Civilization and Its Discontents.

title: Simple Passion
author: Annie Ernaux
tagline: Ernaux documents an all-consuming affair with a married man in clinically precise prose.
description: In fewer than eighty pages, Ernaux records the obsession, waiting, and derangement of desire — stripping away literary artifice to leave only the raw facts of longing. "I accumulated signs of this passion as one might collect evidence."

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Ernaux writes desire as a bodily occupation — waiting by the phone, unable to eat, unable to think. The body is commandeered by passion, and Ernaux records it with forensic precision.

title: Socialism: A Very Short Introduction
author: Michael Newman
tagline: A concise guide to socialism's history, varieties, and prospects — from Marx to the present.
description: Newman traces the development of socialist thought and practice from the utopian socialists through Marx, social democracy, Soviet communism, and the New Left, asking what socialism means in the twenty-first century.

title: Speculative Aesthetics
author: Various
tagline: Essays on the relationship between speculative philosophy, art, and aesthetics after the "speculative turn."
description: A collection exploring how recent developments in speculative realism and object-oriented ontology reshape our understanding of art, beauty, and aesthetic experience.

title: Speed and Politics
author: Paul Virilio
tagline: Virilio argues that political power is fundamentally about speed — whoever controls the pace of life controls the world.
description: A short, dense polemic that traces the relationship between speed, war, and political domination from the ancient city to the nuclear age. Virilio shows how the acceleration of transport, communication, and weapons has reshaped the nature of power itself.

title: Staying with the Trouble
author: Donna Haraway
tagline: Haraway argues for "making kin" across species — staying with the trouble rather than fleeing to techno-fixes.
description: Against both techno-optimism and apocalyptic despair, Haraway proposes "sympoiesis" — making-with, living-with, dying-with across species boundaries. She draws on string figures, science fiction, and the biology of symbiosis to imagine a liveable future on a damaged planet.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Haraway refuses both despair and false hope. Her "staying with the trouble" is a form of resilience that doesn't look away from damage but makes new kinds of life possible within it.

title: Stephen Shore: Uncommon Places
author: Stephen Shore
tagline: The large-format colour photographs that taught a generation to see the beauty in ordinary American places.
description: Shore drove across America in the 1970s with an 8x10 camera, photographing intersections, gas stations, diners, and motel rooms with a clarity and attention to colour that transformed the banal into the luminous. Uncommon Places is a foundational text of colour photography — proof that everyday life, seen with sufficient precision, becomes extraordinary.

themeNotes.Place and Belonging: Shore's photographs insist that the most ordinary American places — parking lots, strip malls, roadside diners — are worthy of the same attention as grand landscapes.

title: Story of O
author: Pauline Réage
tagline: The infamous French novel of submission, desire, and the total surrender of the self.
description: O, a fashion photographer, is taken by her lover to a château where she undergoes systematic sexual subjugation. Written by a woman under a pseudonym in 1954, it scandalised France and remains one of the most controversial explorations of desire, power, and consent in literature.

title: Street & Studio: An Urban History of Photography
author: Ute Eskildsen
tagline: The two poles of photography — the controlled studio and the unpredictable street — explored through a century of images.
description: This Tate catalogue traces the tension between studio portraiture and street photography from the nineteenth century to the present. The book argues that the history of photography is shaped by the oscillation between these two modes: the controlled, the spontaneous; the posed, the caught; the artificial, the real.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: The tension between street and studio is a tension between different forms of photographic power — the street photographer takes, the studio photographer controls.

title: Superagency
author: Reid Hoffman & Greg Beato
tagline: Hoffman on how AI will amplify human agency — the optimistic case for artificial intelligence.
description: LinkedIn co-founder Reid Hoffman argues that AI is not a threat to human autonomy but a tool for expanding it — enabling individuals and organisations to act with unprecedented speed, creativity, and impact.

title: Symbolic Misery (Volume 2)
author: Bernard Stiegler
tagline: Stiegler on the catastrophe of the symbolic — how industrial culture destroys the capacity for aesthetic experience.
description: Stiegler argues that mass media and consumer culture produce a "symbolic misery" — the loss of the ability to feel, to participate in the creation of symbols, and to share aesthetic experience. The result is a population of spectators unable to individuate.

title: Talking Art 2
author: Various (Art Monthly)
tagline: Artists in conversation — studio visits, interviews, and arguments from the pages of Art Monthly.
description: The second volume of interviews from Art Monthly magazine, collecting conversations with artists, critics, and curators working in Britain and internationally. A valuable record of how contemporary artists talk about their own work — the practical, intellectual, and often contradictory thinking that happens in the studio.

title: Tantra: Enlightenment to Revolution
author: Imma Ramos
tagline: The British Museum's survey of Tantra — sacred art, radical philosophy, and the transgression of every boundary.
description: This exhibition catalogue traces Tantra from its fifth-century origins to its influence on modern art, covering sacred diagrams, erotic sculpture, goddess worship, and the radical philosophical proposition that liberation lies in embracing what orthodox religion rejects. Ramos shows Tantra as a tradition that repeatedly overturns hierarchy, purity, and convention.

title: Tao Te Ching
author: Lao Tzu
tagline: Eighty-one short chapters on the Way — stillness, yielding, and the power of not-doing.
description: The foundational text of Taoism. In paradoxical, enigmatic verses, Lao Tzu describes the Tao — the Way that cannot be spoken — and the practice of wu wei, effortless action. "The Tao that can be told is not the eternal Tao."

title: Teaching to Transgress: Education as the Practice of Freedom
author: bell hooks
tagline: hooks reimagines the classroom as a site of liberation — education not as banking but as mutual transformation.
description: Drawing on Paulo Freire, Buddhist practice, and her own experience as a Black woman in the academy, hooks argues for an 'engaged pedagogy' in which teachers and students are transformed together. The classroom becomes a space where hierarchies are challenged, emotional honesty is valued, and education serves freedom rather than conformity.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: hooks writes as a Black woman who experienced education as both liberation and exclusion — her pedagogy is built on the recognition that the classroom is always already a political space.
themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: hooks insists that education can be a practice of freedom even within institutions designed to reproduce inequality — resilience as pedagogical strategy.

title: The Argonauts
author: Maggie Nelson
tagline: A genre-breaking memoir about desire, gender, motherhood, and theory.
description: Nelson writes about her relationship with the artist Harry Dodge — who is transitioning — while she is pregnant, weaving in Barthes, Wittgenstein, Judith Butler, and Eve Kosofsky Sedgwick. It's a book about how language both creates and fails to capture the people we love.

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Two bodies in transformation — one through pregnancy, one through transition. Nelson shows that the body is never fixed but always becoming, and that love is the willingness to accompany that becoming.

title: The Art of Participation: 1950 to Now
author: Various
tagline: The SFMOMA exhibition catalogue on participatory and socially engaged art.
description: A survey of art that requires the viewer's participation — from Fluxus happenings to relational aesthetics to contemporary social practice, asking what happens when the audience becomes the artist.

title: The Artist's Way
author: Julia Cameron
tagline: Cameron's twelve-week course in creative recovery — morning pages, artist dates, and learning to let go.
description: The most widely used creativity course in the world. Cameron's central tools — morning pages (three pages of longhand stream-of-consciousness writing every day) and the artist date (a solo outing to fill the creative well) — have helped millions of blocked creatives start making work again.

title: The Beautiful Risk of Education
author: Gert Biesta
tagline: Biesta argues that real education is risky — it cannot be controlled, measured, or guaranteed.
description: Against the culture of educational measurement and accountability, Biesta argues that the most important things education does — creating subjects, enabling freedom, fostering responsibility — are inherently unpredictable and cannot be reduced to outcomes.

title: The Book of Life
author: Jiddu Krishnamurti
tagline: Daily meditations from Krishnamurti — on freedom, fear, love, and the nature of thought.
description: Three hundred and sixty-five passages drawn from Krishnamurti's talks and writings, organised for daily contemplation. Each entry addresses the fundamental questions of human existence with Krishnamurti's characteristic directness.

title: The Book You Wish Your Parents Had Read
author: Philippa Perry
tagline: Perry on how to break the cycle of inherited parenting patterns — with warmth, honesty, and no guilt trips.
description: A psychotherapist explains how our own childhood experiences shape how we parent, and how to do better without beating ourselves up about it. Practical, compassionate, and grounded in attachment theory.

title: The Complete Tales and Poems
author: Edgar Allan Poe
tagline: Every tale and poem by the master of the macabre — from 'The Raven' to 'The Fall of the House of Usher.'
description: Poe invented the detective story, perfected the horror tale, and wrote some of the most haunting poetry in the English language. This complete collection gathers every story and poem: 'The Murders in the Rue Morgue,' 'The Masque of the Red Death,' 'The Pit and the Pendulum,' 'Annabel Lee,' and scores more. The essential Poe.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Poe's tales are obsessed with what lies beneath — buried alive, bricked up behind walls, hidden beneath floorboards. The repressed always returns, and it returns with a heartbeat.

title: The Conscious Parent
author: Shefali Tsabary
tagline: Tsabary argues that parenting is primarily a spiritual practice — children are our greatest teachers.
description: Drawing on Eastern philosophy and Western psychology, Tsabary proposes that conscious parenting means using the parent-child relationship as a vehicle for the parent's own growth — letting go of control, meeting the child as they are, and recognising your own projections.

title: The Constance Spry Cookery Book
author: Constance Spry & Rosemary Hume
tagline: The definitive mid-century British cookery book — encyclopaedic and enduringly practical.
description: First published in 1956, this mammoth volume was the bible of post-war British cooking. Constance Spry, the legendary flower arranger and domestic arts educator, teamed with Rosemary Hume of the Cordon Bleu school to produce a comprehensive guide covering everything from basic stocks to elaborate dinner party menus. The recipes are classical — soufflés, terrines, roasts, sauces — but the voice is surprisingly modern: opinionated, practical, and often very funny. Spry invented Coronation Chicken for the Queen's coronation in 1953, and the recipe is here alongside hundreds of others that defined an era of British entertaining.

title: The Continental Aesthetics Reader
author: Clive Cazeaux (ed.)
tagline: The essential anthology of continental philosophy of art — from Kant and Hegel through Heidegger, Adorno, and Derrida.
description: A comprehensive reader covering the major figures and movements in continental aesthetics: German Idealism, phenomenology, hermeneutics, critical theory, structuralism, post-structuralism, and contemporary debates.

title: The Cooking of Vienna's Empire
author: Joseph Wechsberg (Time-Life)
tagline: A culinary tour through the Austro-Hungarian Empire, from pastries to goulash.
description: Part of the classic Time-Life Foods of the World series, this volume explores the kitchens of the old Austro-Hungarian Empire — from the coffeehouses of Vienna to the farmhouses of Hungary, Bohemia, and the Balkans. Joseph Wechsberg, a Czech-born writer and gastronome, guides the reader through Sachertorte, Wiener Schnitzel, Hungarian goulash, Czech dumplings, and the elaborate pastry traditions that made Viennese baking legendary. Richly illustrated and written with warmth and authority, it captures a vanished culinary world where empires were held together partly by what people ate.

title: The Covenant of Water
author: Abraham Verghese
tagline: Three generations of a Kerala family bound by a mysterious curse — someone in each generation dies by drowning.
description: Spanning from 1900 to 1977, Verghese's epic follows a family in Kerala through colonialism, independence, and social upheaval, all shadowed by an inherited condition that means at least one member of each generation will drown. Medicine, love, and the weight of family history intertwine.

title: The Cultural Turn
author: Fredric Jameson
tagline: Jameson's selected writings on postmodernity — architecture, film, and the end of art.
description: Essays from the 1980s and 1990s in which Jameson applies his Marxist cultural theory to the Bonaventure Hotel, David Lynch, and the crisis of historicity in the postmodern era.

title: The Culture Industry
author: Theodor W. Adorno
tagline: Adorno's essays on how mass culture manufactures conformity disguised as choice.
description: The culture industry produces the illusion of individuality while standardising everything. These essays — on jazz, television, astrology, and free time — argue that entertainment is not harmless pleasure but a mechanism of social control.

title: The Doctor's Kitchen
author: Dr. Rupy Aujla
tagline: An NHS doctor's guide to eating for health — over 100 recipes backed by nutritional science.
description: Dr. Aujla combines medical knowledge with practical recipes to show how food can be used to support health, reduce inflammation, and prevent chronic disease.

title: The Dreamer's Dictionary
author: Various
tagline: A reference guide to dream symbols and their meanings.
description: An A–Z of dream imagery — from animals to water to flying — offering interpretations drawn from psychoanalytic, Jungian, and folk traditions.

title: The End of Everything
author: Katie Mack
tagline: An astrophysicist walks through five ways the universe might end — with clarity, wit, and genuine wonder.
description: Heat death, the Big Crunch, the Big Rip, vacuum decay, and the Big Bounce — Mack explains each possible ending of the universe with scientific rigour and an infectious sense of awe at the cosmic scales involved.

title: The Essentials of Vedic Astrology
author: Komilla Sutton
tagline: An introduction to Jyotish — the ancient Indian system of astrology and its philosophical foundations.
description: Sutton explains the principles of Vedic astrology — the nakshatras, planetary periods, and the relationship between cosmic cycles and human experience — making an ancient and complex system accessible to Western readers.

title: The EY Exhibition: Picasso 1932
author: Tate
tagline: The Tate exhibition catalogue for Picasso's extraordinary year — Love, Fame, Tragedy.
description: A month-by-month account of 1932, the year Picasso produced some of his most sensual and disturbing work — driven by his secret affair with Marie-Thérèse Walter and his fear of obsolescence.

title: The Famished Road
author: Ben Okri
tagline: A spirit child navigates chaos and beauty in this Booker Prize-winning novel.
description: Azaro is an abiku — a spirit child caught between the world of the living and the world of the unborn, constantly tempted to return to the spirit realm. He chooses to stay in the living world, in a chaotic, unnamed Nigerian city on the brink of independence, where poverty, corruption, and beauty coexist in hallucinatory intensity. Okri creates a narrative that moves fluidly between the everyday and the mythic — political rallies blur into spirit carnivals, a boxing match becomes a cosmic battle. The novel is dense, overwhelming, and extraordinary — a work of African magical realism that creates its own category. Winner of the 1991 Booker Prize.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Azaro sees the spirit world that exists beneath and alongside the physical one. Political corruption, poverty, and violence are shown to have a mythic dimension — the "famished road" of the title is a hunger that runs through reality itself.
themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Azaro chooses to stay in the living world despite its suffering. In a nation on the brink of independence, surrounded by chaos, the act of remaining — of refusing to return to the spirit realm — is itself a form of radical resilience.

title: The Fear of Freedom
author: Erich Fromm
tagline: Fromm asks why people flee from freedom into authoritarianism, conformity, and destructiveness.
description: Written in 1941 as fascism consumed Europe, Fromm argues that modern freedom — liberation from feudal bonds — created a psychic vacuum that people fill with submission to authority, compulsive conformity, or destructive rage. Still devastatingly relevant.

title: The Gay Science
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
tagline: Nietzsche's most joyful and personal work — "God is dead" appears here first.
description: Aphorisms, poems, and experiments in thought that range from the death of God to the eternal return, from the will to power to the "gay" (joyful) science of living without metaphysical comfort. The book where Nietzsche is most himself.

title: The God of Small Things
author: Arundhati Roy
tagline: A luminous story of forbidden love and the small things that shape lives in Kerala.
description: In the town of Ayemenem in Kerala, fraternal twins Rahel and Estha navigate the wreckage of their family — a divorce, a forbidden love affair, a death by drowning, and the rigid caste system that determines who can be loved and how. Roy's prose is extraordinary: lyrical, furious, and precise, moving between the children's perspective and the adults' world with devastating effect. The novel unfolds backwards, revealing its central tragedy slowly, so that when it arrives, the reader understands not just what happened but how the "Love Laws" — "the laws that lay down who should be loved, and how. And how much" — made it inevitable. Winner of the 1997 Booker Prize.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Roy's "Love Laws" — the unwritten rules of caste that determine who may be loved and how much — are the engine of this novel's tragedy. The Untouchable Velutha is destroyed not for what he does but for who he is.
themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: The forbidden relationship at the heart of the novel is punished through bodies — beaten, drowned, separated. Caste is enforced not through abstract rules but through the physical control of who may touch whom.

title: The Golden Mole
author: Catherine Rundell
tagline: Short essays on extraordinary animals — the narwhal, the swift, the golden mole — and why their survival matters.
description: Rundell writes about the animal kingdom with wonder and urgency, weaving natural history with personal reflection to argue that our fate is inseparable from the creatures we share the planet with.
