title: The Hermeneutics of the Subject
author: Michel Foucault
tagline: Foucault's lectures on the ancient practice of "care of the self" — how self-knowledge became self-discipline.
description: In these 1981-82 Collège de France lectures, Foucault traces how the ancient Greek injunction to "know yourself" was originally inseparable from practices of self-care — spiritual exercises, meditation, confession. He shows how Christianity and modernity stripped self-knowledge from self-care, creating the modern subject.

title: The Hero with a Thousand Faces
author: Joseph Campbell
tagline: Campbell's study of the monomyth — the universal pattern underlying all hero stories across cultures.
description: Campbell argues that the myths of every culture share a single underlying structure — the hero's journey: departure, initiation, return. Drawing on Freud, Jung, and worldwide mythology, he traces this pattern from the Buddha to Odysseus to Christ.

title: The Human Condition
author: Hannah Arendt
tagline: Arendt's analysis of labour, work, and action — the three fundamental activities that define human life on earth.
description: Arendt distinguishes between labour (the biological cycle of survival), work (the fabrication of a durable human world), and action (the capacity to begin something new in the public realm). She argues that modernity has elevated labour over action, reducing politics to economics and public life to mere survival.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Arendt argues that the public realm — the space of appearance where people see and are seen by others — is the foundation of political life. When this space is destroyed, power becomes invisible and unaccountable.

title: The Illustrated Book of Mushrooms and Fungi
author: Various
tagline: A visual guide to the fungal kingdom — identification plates, habitat notes, and the strange beauty of spore-bearing organisms.
description: An illustrated reference guide to mushrooms and fungi, with colour plates showing species in their natural habitats. Useful for identification and beautiful as an object — the kind of natural history book that rewards browsing as much as consulting.

title: The Information Bomb
author: Paul Virilio
tagline: Virilio on the weaponisation of information and the dangers of real-time global communication.
description: Virilio argues that the "information bomb" — the instantaneous global transmission of data — is as destructive as the atomic bomb, eliminating the time needed for reflection, deliberation, and democratic decision-making.

title: The Interface Effect
author: Alexander Galloway
tagline: Galloway on how interfaces — screens, windows, doorways — structure our relationship to power and information.
description: Galloway argues that the interface is not a transparent window onto data but an active agent that shapes what can be seen, known, and done. Drawing on media theory and philosophy, he develops a politics of the interface.

title: The Interior Castle
author: St. Teresa of Ávila
tagline: Teresa's account of the soul's journey through seven mansions toward union with God.
description: Written in 1577, The Interior Castle describes the soul as a crystal castle with seven concentric dwellings. The mystic moves inward through prayer, self-knowledge, and suffering until reaching the innermost mansion where the soul unites with God. One of the great texts of Christian mysticism.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Teresa maps the hidden architecture of the soul — seven mansions beneath the surface of ordinary consciousness, each deeper and more luminous than the last. The ultimate journey inward.

title: The Invention of Tradition
author: Eric Hobsbawm & Terence Ranger
tagline: How traditions that appear ancient are often quite recent inventions.
description: This collection of essays demonstrates that many "traditions" we assume to be ancient were actually invented relatively recently — often in the nineteenth century — to serve political purposes. The Highland tradition of Scotland (including tartan and clan identity), the British monarchy's elaborate ceremonies, and colonial rituals in Africa are all shown to be deliberate constructions designed to establish continuity with a suitable past and to legitimate present authority. Hobsbawm's introduction provides the theoretical framework, and the case studies — by historians including Hugh Trevor-Roper and Terence Ranger — are consistently surprising and illuminating.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Hobsbawm reveals that what appears to be timeless tradition is often recent fabrication designed to legitimate power. The kilt, the royal ceremony, the colonial ritual — all invented to create the illusion of ancient authority. Seeing through the invention is an act of intellectual liberation.

title: The Kindred of the Kibbo Kift
author: Annebella Pollen
tagline: The bizarre, beautiful story of Britain's most visionary outdoors movement — woodcraft, pageantry, and utopian politics.
description: The Kibbo Kift was a breakaway movement from the Scouts, founded in 1920 by John Hargrave, that combined camping, handicrafts, and Saxon pageantry with pacifism, world peace, and social credit economics. Pollen's richly illustrated study rescues this extraordinary movement from obscurity — its banners, costumes, and ceremonies are genuine outsider art.

title: The Kitchen Diaries
author: Nigel Slater
tagline: A year in Nigel Slater's kitchen — intimate, seasonal food writing at its best.
description: A diary of a year's cooking, from January to December, recording what Nigel Slater cooked and ate each day. It's deceptively simple — a roast chicken here, a bowl of soup there — but the cumulative effect is a portrait of a life lived in close attention to the seasons. Spring brings asparagus and new potatoes; summer is all tomatoes and stone fruit; autumn means mushrooms and game; winter calls for slow braises and baked puddings. Slater writes about food the way a poet writes about weather — noticing everything, never forcing it, always finding exactly the right word.

title: The Lacanian Subject
author: Bruce Fink
tagline: The clearest introduction to Lacan's theory of the subject — for those who've tried and failed to read Lacan directly.
description: Fink translates Lacan's notoriously difficult concepts — the Other, jouissance, the Real, the divided subject — into accessible prose, making the case that Lacanian psychoanalysis offers a fundamentally different understanding of human experience.

title: The Language of Things
author: Deyan Sudjic
tagline: Sudjic on how designed objects communicate — status, identity, desire, and power.
description: The director of the Design Museum explores how objects speak to us — from luxury goods to military hardware to Apple products — and how design shapes the way we understand ourselves and our world.

title: The Lost Spirit of Capitalism
author: Bernard Stiegler
tagline: Stiegler diagnoses the exhaustion of consumer capitalism and argues for a new political economy of contribution.
description: Building on Max Weber's thesis about capitalism's "spirit," Stiegler argues that this spirit has been destroyed by capitalism itself — replaced by drive-based consumption that produces addiction, depression, and ecological collapse.

title: The Meaning of Things
author: A.C. Grayling
tagline: Short philosophical essays on the concepts that shape everyday life — from courage to death to love.
description: Grayling writes accessible, elegant essays on the ideas we live by — beauty, ambition, sorrow, hope — drawing on philosophy, literature, and history to illuminate what these concepts actually mean in practice.

title: The Moosewood Cookbook
author: Mollie Katzen
tagline: The hand-lettered classic that brought vegetarian cooking into the mainstream.
description: Originally published in 1977 and hand-lettered by Katzen herself, The Moosewood Cookbook emerged from the collectively run Moosewood Restaurant in Ithaca, New York, and became one of the best-selling cookbooks of all time. The recipes — mushroom-barley soup, spanakopita, tahini dressing, baba ganoush — now feel like old friends, but at the time they were revolutionary: proof that meatless food could be hearty, satisfying, and full of flavour. The charming hand-drawn illustrations and handwritten recipes give it a warmth that no digital cookbook has matched.

title: The Mushroom at the End of the World
author: Anna Lowenhaupt Tsing
tagline: Following the matsutake mushroom through ruined landscapes.
description: The matsutake mushroom is one of the most valuable fungi in the world, prized in Japan and found growing in the ruins of industrial forests. Tsing follows it from the devastated landscapes of Oregon to the auction houses of Tokyo, using it as a guide to understanding life in the ruins of capitalism. Along the way, she encounters Hmong and Lao refugees who pick the mushrooms, Japanese traders who evaluate them by smell and feel, and ecologists studying how forests regenerate after destruction. It's a radical work of anthropology that argues for a new way of thinking about survival, collaboration, and what comes after progress.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Tsing's central insight: the matsutake mushroom thrives in devastated forests, creating value in landscapes that capitalism has used up and abandoned. Life doesn't need pristine conditions — it finds ways to flourish in the ruins.

title: The New Vegan
author: Áine Carlin
tagline: Accessible vegan cooking for beginners — simple, delicious, and unpretentious.
description: Carlin's debut cookbook makes vegan cooking approachable, with over 100 recipes that don't require specialist ingredients or complicated techniques.

title: The Nineties: When Surface was Depth
author: Michael Bracewell
tagline: A cultural history of 1990s Britain told through pop, art, fashion, and the strange euphoria of the end of history.
description: Bracewell captures the mood of nineties Britain — Britpop, YBAs, New Labour, the millennium dome — with the eye of a novelist and the sensibility of a cultural critic. He argues that the decade's obsession with surface, style, and irony was not superficiality but a new form of depth: a culture processing the end of ideology through pop.

title: The Other Side: A Journey into Women, Art and the Spirit World
author: Jennifer Higgie
tagline: Women artists who communed with spirits — from Hilma af Klint to Leonora Carrington, mysticism as creative practice.
description: Higgie traces a hidden history of women artists whose work was shaped by engagement with the spirit world: séances, automatic drawing, visionary experience, and esoteric practice. From Hilma af Klint to Remedios Varo, from Georgiana Houghton to Leonora Carrington, she recovers a tradition in which mysticism was not a retreat from art but its deepest source.

themeNotes.Consciousness and the Sacred: Higgie's artists experienced the spirit world as real — their art was not illustration of belief but a record of genuine visionary encounter.
themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Women's engagement with the occult was often dismissed as hysteria or superstition — Higgie reclaims it as a serious creative and intellectual tradition.

title: The Outsider
author: Albert Camus
tagline: Meursault kills an Arab on an Algerian beach and refuses to play the game of social convention.
description: Camus's first novel is a study in alienation. Meursault does not cry at his mother's funeral, begins a casual affair the next day, and shoots a man for no clear reason. His real crime, it turns out, is his refusal to perform the emotions society demands. A founding text of existentialism and absurdism.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Meursault is condemned not for murder but for his failure to perform acceptable grief, love, and remorse. Camus shows how society punishes those who refuse to wear its emotional masks.

title: The Painting of Modern Life: 1960s to Now
author: Ralph Rugoff
tagline: Painting's stubborn survival — from Pop to the present, the medium that refuses to die.
description: This Hayward Gallery catalogue argues that painting, far from being killed off by conceptualism and new media, has remained a vital and unpredictable art form. The exhibition traced a line from 1960s Pop through photorealism, Neo-Expressionism, and the return to figuration, arguing that painting's relationship to everyday life has kept it perpetually relevant.

title: The Photobook: A History Volume I
author: Martin Parr & Gerry Badger
tagline: The definitive history of the photobook — from Fox Talbot to the 1960s, the book as photography's natural home.
description: Parr and Badger argue that the photobook — not the gallery print — is the primary vehicle for photographic expression. This first volume traces the history from the earliest photographic publications through the great mid-century photobooks of Robert Frank, William Klein, and Ed Ruscha. Lavishly illustrated and impeccably curated, it transformed how people thought about photography's relationship to the printed page.

title: The Photobook: A History Volume II
author: Martin Parr & Gerry Badger
tagline: The second volume — from the 1960s to the present, the photobook's explosion into art, politics, and self-publishing.
description: Continuing from where Volume I ended, Parr and Badger survey the photobook from the 1960s to the digital age, covering Japanese photography, conceptual art books, political documentation, and the contemporary self-publishing boom. Together with Volume I, this constitutes the essential reference work on photography's most important format.

title: The Photograph as Contemporary Art
author: Charlotte Cotton
tagline: A survey of how contemporary artists use photography — from staged tableaux to appropriation to the post-internet image.
description: Cotton maps the field of art photography since the 1980s, organised by approach: deadpan, intimate, appropriated, staged, digital. A clear-eyed guide to how photography became contemporary art's dominant medium.

title: The Plague
author: Jacqueline Rose
tagline: Rose reads the pandemic through psychoanalysis, feminism, and the politics of illness — who gets to be sick and who must care.
description: Written during COVID, Rose argues that pandemics reveal the fault lines of society — who is expendable, who does the caring, and how illness exposes the cruelty that "normal" life conceals. She draws on Freud, Camus, and her own experience of lockdown.

title: The Pleasure of the Text
author: Roland Barthes
tagline: Barthes on the erotics of reading — the difference between texts of pleasure and texts of bliss.
description: A slim, sensual book that treats reading as a bodily experience. Barthes distinguishes between texts that satisfy (pleasure) and texts that unsettle, that bring the reader to the edge of language (bliss/jouissance).

title: The Primacy of Perception
author: Maurice Merleau-Ponty
tagline: Merleau-Ponty's essays on embodied perception — the body as our primary way of knowing the world.
description: Against the Cartesian split between mind and body, Merleau-Ponty argues that perception is not a mental act but a bodily one. We know the world through our flesh, not through abstract thought. These essays develop his phenomenology of embodiment across aesthetics, politics, and science.

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Merleau-Ponty insists that the body is not an object we have but the subject we are. All perception, all thought, all knowledge begins in the lived body — making embodiment the foundation of everything.

title: The Quadruple Object
author: Graham Harman
tagline: Harman's concise statement of object-oriented ontology — the fourfold structure of every object.
description: Every object, Harman argues, has four dimensions: its real qualities, its sensual qualities, its real object, and its sensual object. This fourfold structure means that objects always withdraw from full access — by humans or by each other.

title: The Remains of the Day
author: Kazuo Ishiguro
tagline: A butler reflects on service and the life he chose not to live.
description: Stevens, the impeccable butler of Darlington Hall, takes a motoring trip through the English countryside in 1956, ostensibly to visit a former colleague, Miss Kenton. As he drives, he reflects on his decades of service to Lord Darlington — a man who, it gradually becomes clear, was a Nazi sympathiser. Stevens's narration is a masterclass in self-deception: every memory he shares reveals more than he intends about his complicity, his loneliness, and the love he never acknowledged. Ishiguro builds the novel like a piece of music, each revelation arriving with quiet, devastating precision. Winner of the 1989 Booker Prize.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Everything important in this novel happens beneath what Stevens says. His dignity is a mask. His loyalty is complicity. His "professional" distance from Miss Kenton is suppressed love. Ishiguro creates a character whose entire life is a surface concealing what lies beneath.

title: The Russian Linesman
author: Mark Wallinger
tagline: Wallinger's Hayward Gallery exhibition catalogue — art about borders, identity, and the contested line.
description: Named after the 1966 World Cup goal-line controversy, Wallinger's exhibition explored boundaries of all kinds — national borders, the line between sanity and madness, the threshold between art and non-art.

title: The Second Sex
author: Simone de Beauvoir
tagline: The foundational text of modern feminism — still radical after all these years.
description: "One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman." With that sentence, Beauvoir launched modern feminist philosophy. The Second Sex is a comprehensive examination of women's oppression, drawing on biology, psychoanalysis, historical materialism, literature, and lived experience to argue that femininity is not a natural fact but a social construction. Beauvoir traces how women have been defined as the "Other" throughout Western history and analyses how this otherness is reproduced in childhood, education, marriage, and motherhood. Published in 1949, it remains the single most important work of feminist theory — dense, encyclopaedic, and still capable of provoking argument.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Beauvoir's central argument: woman has been constructed as man's "Other" — not a subject in her own right but a mirror for male identity. The entire apparatus of femininity is a system for producing and maintaining this otherness.
themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Beauvoir traces how the female body — menstruation, pregnancy, ageing — is used to justify women's subordination. The body is not destiny, she argues, but it has been made into a cage by a society that reads biology as fate.

title: The Seminar: The Formations of the Unconscious
author: Jacques Lacan
tagline: Lacan's 1957–58 seminar on jokes, the unconscious, and the structure of desire.
description: Lacan reads Freud's Jokes and Their Relation to the Unconscious through structuralist linguistics, developing his theory that the unconscious is structured like a language. The seminar also introduces the graph of desire.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Lacan's entire project is an investigation of what lies beneath — the unconscious structured like a language, operating beneath conscious speech, shaping desire without the subject's knowledge.

title: The Seven Spiritual Laws of Success
author: Deepak Chopra
tagline: Chopra distils Vedantic philosophy into seven principles for living with effortless ease.
description: Drawing on Hindu and Buddhist thought, Chopra presents seven laws — from the Law of Pure Potentiality to the Law of Dharma — as a practical framework for aligning with the flow of life rather than fighting against it.

title: The Song of Songs
author: Iain M. Duguid
tagline: A commentary on the Bible's most erotic and enigmatic book — love poetry as theology.
description: Duguid reads the Song of Songs both as a celebration of human erotic love and as an allegory of the relationship between God and his people, navigating centuries of competing interpretations with scholarly care.

title: The Story of Art
author: E.H. Gombrich
tagline: The most widely read introduction to art history ever written — clear, humane, and still indispensable.
description: Gombrich's survey — first published in 1950 and revised through sixteen editions — traces the history of art from cave painting to the twentieth century with a clarity and warmth that has never been surpassed. His central insight — that there is no such thing as 'Art,' only artists — grounds a narrative that treats each period's visual solutions as rational responses to specific problems.

title: The Surrender Experiment
author: Michael A. Singer
tagline: Singer describes what happened when he stopped resisting life and let events unfold.
description: After a meditation experience in the 1970s, Singer decided to stop following his personal preferences and simply say yes to whatever life presented. The result: he built a billion-dollar software company, nearly went to prison, and discovered that surrender — not control — is the path to a meaningful life.

title: The System of Objects
author: Jean Baudrillard
tagline: Baudrillard's early analysis of how consumer objects organise our lives and desires.
description: Before Baudrillard became the theorist of simulation, he wrote this semiotic analysis of consumer culture — how objects are arranged, displayed, and consumed not for their use but for their sign value.

title: The Tantric Way
author: Mookerjee & Khanna
tagline: A richly illustrated exploration of Tantric art, philosophy, and practice.
description: This classic text introduces the principles of Tantra through its art and iconography — yantras, mandalas, ritual objects, and temple sculpture — showing how the Tantric tradition integrates body, mind, and cosmos.

title: The Tao of Physics
author: Fritjof Capra
tagline: Capra draws parallels between modern physics and Eastern mysticism.
description: First published in 1975, Capra argues that the worldview emerging from quantum physics and relativity resonates deeply with Hindu, Buddhist, and Taoist thought — both traditions pointing toward an interconnected, dynamic reality that defies mechanistic thinking.

title: The Tao of Pooh & The Te of Piglet
author: Benjamin Hoff
tagline: Hoff explains Taoist philosophy through Winnie-the-Pooh — the Uncarved Block, the Power of the Small.
description: Pooh is the Taoist master who doesn't try: he simply is. Hoff uses A.A. Milne's characters to illuminate the principles of Taoism with warmth, humour, and surprising depth.

title: The Three Ecologies
author: Félix Guattari
tagline: Guattari argues that environmental, social, and mental ecologies are inseparable — you cannot save the planet without transforming subjectivity.
description: A short, fierce polemic arguing that ecological crisis is also a crisis of subjectivity and social relations. Guattari proposes "ecosophy" — an ethico-political articulation of the three registers of ecology: environmental, social, and mental.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Guattari writes from the ruins of both capitalism and orthodox Marxism, arguing that genuine ecological resilience requires not just saving forests but remaking how we think, feel, and relate.

title: The Tibetan Book of the Dead
author: Padmasambhava (trans. various)
tagline: The Buddhist guide to the bardos — the intermediate states between death and rebirth.
description: Traditionally read aloud to the dying and the recently dead, this text describes the experiences consciousness undergoes after death — the clear light, the peaceful and wrathful deities, and the process of rebirth. A manual for navigating the most radical transformation a being can undergo.

title: The Undercommons
author: Stefano Harney & Fred Moten
tagline: A radical text on fugitive planning, Black study, and the politics of refusal.
description: The Undercommons argues that the university, far from being a space of free inquiry, is a site of governance and regulation — and that genuine intellectual life happens in the "undercommons," the informal networks of study, friendship, and mutual aid that exist beneath and against institutional structures. Harney and Moten draw on Black radical thought, jazz, and critical theory to develop concepts of "fugitive planning" and "hapticality" — ways of being together that refuse the terms of neoliberal governance. It's a book that has become a touchstone for radical thinkers across disciplines, dense and poetic in equal measure.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Harney and Moten argue that the university — supposedly a place of free knowledge — is actually a machine for producing compliant subjects. Genuine study happens fugitively, in the margins, beyond the institution's gaze.
themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: The "undercommons" is a form of survival within hostile institutions — informal networks of care, study, and solidarity that exist beneath and despite the structures designed to govern them. Resilience as refusal.

title: The Upanishads
author: Various (trans. various)
tagline: The ancient Indian philosophical texts that form the basis of Vedanta — on Brahman, Atman, and the nature of reality.
description: The Upanishads are the philosophical core of the Vedas, composed between 800 and 200 BCE. Through dialogue, parable, and meditation, they explore the identity of the individual self (Atman) with the universal ground of being (Brahman). "Tat tvam asi" — "You are that."

title: The Weird and the Eerie
author: Mark Fisher
tagline: Fisher explores the aesthetics of the strange — what is present that shouldn't be, what is absent that should be.
description: The weird is when something is present that should not be — a Lovecraftian entity, a glitch in reality. The eerie is when something is absent that should be there — an abandoned building, an empty landscape. Fisher reads these affects through Lovecraft, Daphne du Maurier, Kubrick, and Joy Division.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Fisher's weird is precisely what erupts from beneath the surface of the normal — the thing that should not be there but is. His eerie is the opposite: the unsettling absence that reveals how thin the surface always was.

title: The Whole-Brain Child: 12 Revolutionary Strategies to Nurture Your Child's Developing Mind
author: Daniel J. Siegel & Tina Payne Bryson
tagline: Neuroscience-informed parenting — how understanding the developing brain transforms discipline and connection.
description: Siegel and Bryson translate neuroscience into practical parenting strategies, showing how children's brains develop and how parents can support integration between the logical left brain and the emotional right brain. Clear, compassionate, and grounded in research, it has become one of the most influential parenting books of the last decade.

title: The Wild Dyer
author: Abigail Booth
tagline: A seasonal guide to dyeing textiles with plants — elderberries, onion skins, weld, and woad.
description: Booth guides the reader through the art of natural dyeing season by season, showing how to extract colour from common plants, create mordants, and produce beautiful, subtle colours from the landscape around you.

title: The Wind-Up Bird Chronicle
author: Haruki Murakami
tagline: A mesmerising labyrinth of memory, violence, and the mysteries of consciousness.
description: Toru Okada's cat has gone missing, and then his wife disappears too. What begins as a domestic mystery spirals into a surreal odyssey through abandoned wells, psychic prostitutes, wartime atrocities in Manchuria, and the dark corridors of the subconscious. Murakami weaves multiple timelines and narratives into a vast, dreamlike novel about the violence that lies beneath the surface of ordinary life. It's his masterpiece — a book where the mundane and the metaphysical coexist so naturally that you stop questioning the boundaries between them.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Murakami's protagonist descends into a dry well and finds that beneath suburban Tokyo lies a labyrinth of wartime violence, suppressed memory, and metaphysical darkness. The novel argues that what we call "ordinary life" is a thin crust over something vast and terrifying.

title: The Woman's Dictionary of Symbols and Sacred Objects
author: Barbara G. Walker
tagline: An encyclopaedia of symbols associated with the feminine — from ancient goddess cultures to modern iconography.
description: Walker catalogues hundreds of symbols, signs, and sacred objects connected to women's spiritual traditions across cultures — moons, spirals, chalices, serpents — arguing that an older, goddess-centred symbolic language persists beneath patriarchal culture.

title: The Work of Charles and Ray Eames
author: Various
tagline: The husband-and-wife team who made modernism playful — chairs, films, exhibitions, and the art of making connections.
description: This volume surveys the Eameses' extraordinarily diverse output: the moulded plywood and fibreglass chairs, the films (Powers of Ten, Toccata for Toy Trains), the exhibition designs, and the Case Study House. Their work embodied a vision of modernism as democratic, accessible, and joyful — design as a way of improving daily life.

title: The World Atlas
author: Various
tagline: A comprehensive atlas — mountains, rivers, borders, and human civilisation.
description: A large-format reference atlas containing detailed physical and political maps of every region of the world, supplemented with thematic maps showing population, climate, vegetation, and economic activity. The kind of book you open to look up one thing and find yourself browsing for an hour, tracing coastlines with your finger, discovering islands you've never heard of, and wondering about the lives lived in places whose names you can barely pronounce. In the age of Google Maps, a physical atlas remains a fundamentally different experience — slower, more contemplative, and more conducive to the kind of wandering that leads to genuine curiosity.

themeNotes.Place and Belonging: An atlas is a map of belonging itself — who lives where, which borders divide them, which rivers connect them. A physical atlas invites a slower, more contemplative relationship with geography than any screen can offer.

title: The World in 2050
author: Hamish McRae
tagline: An economist's forecast of the next twenty-five years — demography, technology, and the shifting balance of power.
description: McRae projects current trends in population, technology, climate, and geopolitics forward to 2050, arguing that the world will be shaped by an ageing West, a rising Asia, and the transformative effects of AI and renewable energy.

title: The Year of Magical Thinking
author: Joan Didion
tagline: Didion's devastating memoir of grief — precise, elegant, and utterly truthful.
description: On the evening of 30 December 2003, Joan Didion and her husband John Gregory Dunne sat down to dinner. Dunne suffered a fatal cardiac arrest at the table. Their daughter Quintana was in a coma in a nearby hospital. Didion's account of the year that followed is written with her characteristic precision — she researches grief the way she researched politics, reading medical journals, analysing her own irrationality, and noting the "magical thinking" that made her believe she could somehow undo the death. The result is both universal and deeply personal: a book about what happens to the mind when the unthinkable happens, written by someone who has spent her life paying attention.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Didion's grief is a personal apocalypse — the complete destruction of the life she knew. The "magical thinking" of the title is itself a form of resilience: the mind's refusal to accept ruin, even as the writer's discipline forces her to document it with absolute precision.

title: Things Fall Apart
author: Chinua Achebe
tagline: The foundational novel of African literature — Okonkwo and the collision with colonialism.
description: Okonkwo is a respected warrior and leader of the Igbo village of Umuofia, a man who has built his life in opposition to his gentle, unsuccessful father. When Christian missionaries arrive and the British colonial government begins to impose its authority, the world Okonkwo knows starts to unravel. Achebe writes with the calm clarity of a historian and the compassion of a novelist, showing both the strengths and tensions within Igbo society before the colonisers came, and the devastating speed with which everything changed. Published in 1958, it remains the most widely read African novel, translated into over fifty languages.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Achebe wrote this novel specifically to counter the colonial narrative that Africa had no culture before Europeans arrived. The Igbo world he depicts is complex, sophisticated, and self-governing — the "other" was never the blank slate colonisers needed it to be.
themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: The title says it all. A complete world — with its own laws, gods, and social order — falls apart under colonial pressure. Achebe records both the collapse and what existed before it, ensuring the ruins carry memory.

title: Thomas Demand: Model Studies
author: Thomas Demand
tagline: Paper reconstructions of real spaces, photographed and destroyed — the uncanny valley between model and reality.
description: Demand builds full-scale paper models of spaces he has seen in photographs — offices, crime scenes, laboratories — then photographs them and destroys the models. The resulting images hover between the real and the artificial, familiar and deeply strange. This two-volume set presents his major series alongside essays on his unique method.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Demand's paper reconstructions strip away every trace of human presence — no fingerprints, no wear, no text — revealing the uncanny emptiness beneath the surface of documentary images.

title: Thomas Struth: Museum Photographs
author: Thomas Struth
tagline: Photographs of people looking at art in museums — the act of looking itself becomes the subject.
description: Struth's museum photographs capture visitors gazing at paintings in the Louvre, the Prado, and the Uffizi. The images create a mise en abyme — we look at photographs of people looking at paintings, and become conscious of our own act of looking. A quietly profound meditation on attention, art, and the social rituals of the museum.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Struth makes visible the apparatus of looking — the museum as a space where power, attention, and aesthetic experience are choreographed.

title: Thought in the Act
author: Brian Massumi & Erin Manning
tagline: Massumi and Manning on thinking through movement, art, and the creative event.
description: A collaboration between a philosopher and a dancer-artist exploring how thought emerges from movement and creative practice — not as reflection after the fact but as an immanent dimension of the act itself.

title: Timaeus and Critias
author: Plato
tagline: Plato's cosmology and the myth of Atlantis — how the universe was made and how civilisations fall.
description: In the Timaeus, Plato presents his account of the creation of the universe by a divine craftsman. In the Critias, he tells the story of Atlantis — the great civilisation destroyed by its own hubris. Philosophy as mythology.

title: Together
author: Ece Temelkuran
tagline: Temelkuran on what it takes to build solidarity in an age of division.
description: The Turkish journalist and political commentator — exiled for her opposition to Erdoğan — writes about the conditions for genuine collective action: trust, dignity, shared vulnerability, and the willingness to be changed by others.

title: Touching Peace
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
tagline: Thich Nhat Hanh on mindfulness as the foundation of peace — in the self, in relationships, and in the world.
description: With characteristic gentleness, Thich Nhat Hanh shows how the practice of mindfulness — conscious breathing, walking meditation, deep listening — can transform suffering into peace, one moment at a time.

title: Twelve Steps and Twelve Traditions
author: Alcoholics Anonymous
tagline: The companion volume to the Big Book — detailed essays on each of the twelve steps and twelve traditions of AA.
description: Written by Bill Wilson in 1953, this book expands on the spiritual principles underlying each step and tradition, offering practical guidance for recovery and for building communities of mutual support.

title: Two Regimes of Madness
author: Gilles Deleuze
tagline: Deleuze's late interviews and short texts — on control societies, Foucault, and the future of philosophy.
description: A collection of Deleuze's texts from 1975 to 1995, including the influential "Postscript on the Societies of Control" and conversations about cinema, literature, and the relationship between philosophy and art.

title: Typologien
author: Bernd & Hilla Becher
tagline: The Bechers' austere taxonomies of industrial architecture — water towers, blast furnaces, winding towers — arranged in grids of deadpan precision.
description: Bernd and Hilla Becher spent decades photographing disappearing industrial structures across Europe and America, always from the same angle, in the same flat light. Arranged in typological grids, these images transform functional architecture into sculptural form. This volume gathers their major series — a monument to a vanishing industrial world.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: The Bechers documented industrial structures already on the verge of demolition — their typologies are acts of preservation, turning obsolescence into art.

title: Undercover Surrealism: Georges Bataille and Documents
author: Dawn Ades & Simon Baker
tagline: The dissident surrealism of Bataille's Documents magazine — ethnography, abjection, and the informe.
description: This Hayward Gallery catalogue reconstructs the world of Documents, the magazine Bataille edited from 1929 to 1930 as a rival to Breton's official Surrealism. Where Breton sought the marvellous, Bataille pursued the base, the formless, and the sacred — publishing photographs of slaughterhouses alongside essays on big toes and spitting. A key text for understanding the dark underside of Surrealism.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Bataille's Documents pursued what official Surrealism repressed — the abject, the formless, and the sacred violence that lurks beneath civilised life.

title: Underland: A Deep Time Journey
author: Robert Macfarlane
tagline: A descent into the worlds beneath our feet.
description: Macfarlane goes underground — into the caves beneath the Mendip Hills, the catacombs of Paris, the potash mines of Yorkshire, the ice cores of Greenland, and the nuclear waste repositories of Finland — to explore what lies beneath. Each descent becomes a meditation on deep time: the vast scales of geological history that dwarf human existence. He encounters mycorrhizal networks that connect forest trees, Bronze Age burial chambers, the "dark matter" laboratories beneath the Italian Alps, and the artists who have painted on cave walls for forty thousand years. It's nature writing at its most ambitious — a book about what the underworld can teach us about time, loss, and the future of the planet.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: The most literal expression of this theme in the collection. Macfarlane descends into caves, mines, catacombs, and nuclear waste stores to discover what the underground world reveals about memory, time, and what we choose to bury.
themeNotes.Place and Belonging: Macfarlane shows that "place" extends downward — beneath every landscape lies another landscape, layered with geological and human history. To truly know where you are, you must understand what lies beneath your feet.

title: Understanding a Photograph
author: John Berger
tagline: Berger's selected writings on photography — seeing as a moral and political act.
description: A companion to Ways of Seeing, focused on photography. Berger writes about Paul Strand, Henri Cartier-Bresson, and the ethics of looking at photographs of suffering, war, and beauty.

title: Understanding Media
author: Marshall McLuhan
tagline: "The medium is the message" — McLuhan's prophetic analysis of how media reshape human consciousness.
description: McLuhan argues that it is not the content of media that matters but the medium itself — television, print, radio each restructure perception, thought, and social organisation. Written in 1964, it predicted the internet age with uncanny accuracy.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: McLuhan showed that media don't just carry messages — they restructure how we perceive reality. The medium is the message: whoever controls the medium controls the shape of thought itself.

title: Urs Fischer
author: Urs Fischer
tagline: Melting wax candles, excavated gallery floors, and the gleeful destruction of art's preciousness.
description: Fischer's work ranges from life-size wax sculptures that slowly melt over the course of an exhibition to entire gallery floors dug up and left as craters. This monograph captures the anarchic energy of an artist for whom destruction, humour, and spectacle are inseparable from creation.

title: Vanessa Beecroft: Performances 1993-2003
author: Marcella Beccaria
tagline: Rows of near-identical women standing motionless in galleries — beauty, discomfort, and the spectacle of the body.
description: Beecroft's performances place groups of women — often nearly nude, in identical wigs and shoes — in gallery spaces where they stand motionless while visitors circulate around them. The work provokes intense discomfort about looking, objectification, and the aestheticisation of the female body. This Skira volume documents a decade of increasingly ambitious performances.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Beecroft's performances make the act of looking at women's bodies unavoidably conscious — you cannot look without feeling the weight of your own gaze.
themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: The uniformity of the bodies, the enforced stillness, the nudity — Beecroft stages the female body as simultaneously powerful and objectified.

title: Violence
author: Slavoj Žižek
tagline: Žižek distinguishes subjective violence (visible acts) from the objective violence that sustains "normal" life.
description: The real violence, Žižek argues, is not the spectacular eruptions we see on the news but the systemic violence embedded in the smooth functioning of capitalism — and the symbolic violence of language that frames how we see the world.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Žižek's central point: the most dangerous violence is invisible — the systemic violence that maintains "normal" social order. What we call peace is itself a form of violence, hidden beneath the surface.

title: Visions of Excess
author: Georges Bataille
tagline: Bataille's early writings on sacrifice, eroticism, and the sacred — transgression as the engine of human experience.
description: Selected writings from the 1920s and 1930s in which Bataille develops his theory of expenditure — the human need to waste, to destroy, to exceed all limits. Essays on the solar anus, the pineal eye, and the notion that the sacred is inseparable from violence.

title: Warhol: Red Books
author: Andy Warhol
tagline: Warhol's personal address books — a social archaeology of the Factory years, name by name.
description: Published by Steidl/Pace/MacGill, this volume reproduces Warhol's actual address books — handwritten entries, crossed-out names, phone numbers of superstars, socialites, dealers, and hangers-on. A fascinatingly intimate document of Warhol's vast social network, presented as the readymade artwork he would have appreciated.

title: Ways of Seeing
author: John Berger
tagline: The revolutionary essay that changed how we look at art.
description: Based on the BBC television series, Ways of Seeing is a series of seven essays — four using words, three using images alone — that fundamentally challenged how art is understood. Berger argues that mechanical reproduction has transformed the meaning of paintings, that the tradition of oil painting is inseparable from property and possession, and that the representation of women in Western art has been shaped by the male gaze. Written with razor clarity and illustrated with carefully chosen juxtapositions, it remains the most influential work of art criticism of the last fifty years. Every page is a provocation.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: The foundational text for this entire theme. Berger demonstrates that the way we see art is shaped by assumptions about property, gender, and class — and that learning to see differently is the first step toward thinking differently about power.

title: What is Madness?
author: Darian Leader
tagline: Leader asks what distinguishes madness from sanity — and argues the boundary is thinner than we think.
description: A Lacanian psychoanalyst argues that the difference between neurosis and psychosis is not about severity but structure, and that understanding madness requires listening to what the mad person is actually saying rather than diagnosing them from the outside.

title: What Is Philosophy?
author: Gilles Deleuze & Félix Guattari
tagline: Philosophy as the creation of concepts — not the search for truth but the invention of new ways of thinking.
description: In their final collaboration, Deleuze and Guattari define philosophy not as reflection or contemplation but as the creation of concepts on a 'plane of immanence.' They distinguish philosophy from science (which creates functions) and art (which creates affects and percepts), arguing that all three modes of thought are equally valuable and irreducible to one another. A summation and farewell.

themeNotes.Consciousness and the Sacred: Deleuze and Guattari treat philosophy itself as a sacred activity — the creation of concepts as an encounter with chaos that requires courage and invention.

title: White Teeth
author: Zadie Smith
tagline: A dazzling debut about families, immigration, and identity in multicultural London.
description: Archie Jones, a working-class Englishman, and Samad Iqbal, a Bengali Muslim, become unlikely best friends after serving together in World War II. Their families — the Joneses, the Iqbals, and the middle-class Chalfens — collide across three decades of London life, navigating race, religion, class, and the comedy of trying to belong. Smith's debut is bursting with energy, ideas, and characters, veering from slapstick to philosophy without missing a beat. It captured multicultural London at the turn of the millennium and announced one of the most significant literary voices of her generation.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Every character in White Teeth is negotiating what it means to belong in a country that isn't sure it wants them. Samad's anguish about raising Muslim children in secular London is the novel's most poignant exploration of otherness as a daily, lived condition.
themeNotes.Place and Belonging: Smith's London is a place where identities are constantly being remade — through intermarriage, through food, through argument. Belonging here isn't about roots but about the ongoing, messy, comic negotiation of sharing a city.

title: Wide Sargasso Sea
author: Jean Rhys
tagline: The haunting prequel to Jane Eyre — a masterpiece of postcolonial literature.
description: Antoinette Cosway grows up in post-emancipation Jamaica, caught between the Black community that distrusts her family and the white colonial class that has rejected them. When she marries an unnamed Englishman — recognisable as Rochester from Jane Eyre — her identity is systematically dismantled. He renames her Bertha, dismisses her Creole heritage, and eventually locks her away. Rhys gives voice to the "madwoman in the attic," revealing the racism, patriarchy, and colonial violence that Brontë left unexamined. Written in prose of tropical intensity, it's one of the great novels of the twentieth century.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Antoinette belongs nowhere — too white for the Black community, too Creole for her English husband. Rhys shows how colonial othering works not just between races but within them, stripping identity down to nothing.
themeNotes.Place and Belonging: Jamaica is a character in this novel — its heat, its colours, its danger. When Antoinette is taken to England, the loss of place is inseparable from the loss of self. Rhys shows that to be uprooted is to be unmade.

title: Willem de Kooning
author: Willem de Kooning
tagline: The abstract expressionist who never fully let go of the figure — women, landscapes, and the fury of paint.
description: De Kooning's canvases — the slashing brushwork of the Women series, the luminous later abstractions — represent Abstract Expressionism at its most physically intense. This monograph traces his career from the early black-and-white paintings through the controversial Women to the serene late works, charting an artist who refused to choose between figuration and abstraction.

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: De Kooning's Women are bodies in violent flux — attacked, dismembered, and reassembled by the brush. The female body is simultaneously celebrated and destroyed.

title: William Eggleston: 5x7
author: William Eggleston
tagline: Intimate, jewel-like colour photographs — the dye-transfer master working at a smaller, more intense scale.
description: Shot on 5x7 film rather than Eggleston's usual 35mm, these photographs have a density and saturation that intensifies his vision of the American South. Tricycles, parking lots, kitchen counters, and empty roads are rendered with the precision and richness of Old Master painting.

themeNotes.Place and Belonging: Eggleston's photographs locate the sacred in the most ordinary Southern landscapes — the beauty of a specific place, seen with absolute clarity.

title: William Eggleston: Paris
author: William Eggleston
tagline: The supreme colourist of the American South turns his camera on Paris — and the city becomes unmistakably his.
description: Eggleston in Paris: café tables, metro signs, shop windows, and the play of light on stone — all rendered in his signature colour palette. The photographs prove that Eggleston's gift is not for any particular place but for colour and attention themselves. Published by Steidl in collaboration with the Fondation Cartier.

title: Wolfgang Tillmans: if one thing matters, everything matters
author: Wolfgang Tillmans
tagline: The photographer who insists that every image — a friend's portrait, a sunset, a crumpled pair of jeans — matters equally.
description: Tillmans's Tate exhibition catalogue presents his distinctive installation method: photographs of every size and subject — clubbers, still lifes, abstractions, political demonstrations — arranged across gallery walls without hierarchy. The result is an argument for the democracy of images and the radical proposition that attention is a form of love.

title: Wolfgang Tillmans: View from Above
author: Wolfgang Tillmans
tagline: Aerial perspectives and the abstract beauty of the world seen from a distance.
description: This Hatje Cantz publication presents Tillmans's photographs taken from above — aerial views, landscapes seen from planes, and abstract compositions that hover between documentation and pure form. The elevated viewpoint transforms the familiar into the abstract.

title: Women in Praise of the Sacred
author: Jane Hirshfield (ed.)
tagline: An anthology of women's spiritual poetry from forty-three centuries — from Enheduanna to Emily Dickinson.
description: Hirshfield gathers poems by women mystics, visionaries, and seekers across cultures and millennia — Sumerian priestesses, Japanese Buddhist nuns, Sufi poets, Christian contemplatives — revealing a continuous tradition of women's sacred expression.

title: Wouldn't It Be Nice... Wishful Thinking in Art and Design
author: Emily King & others
tagline: Utopian proposals and impossible projects — art and design that imagine a better world.
description: This catalogue gathers artists and designers whose work proposes alternatives to the way things are: speculative architectures, impossible products, and visions of social transformation. A survey of creative optimism at a moment when utopian thinking was deeply unfashionable.

title: Xavier Ribas
author: Xavier Ribas
tagline: Photographs of marginal urban spaces — wastelands, edges, and the traces of unofficial inhabitation.
description: Ribas photographs the overlooked peripheries of cities: patches of waste ground used for Sunday picnics, abandoned lots, and the margins between built and unbuilt space. His work is a quiet archaeology of how people use places that planners have forgotten.

themeNotes.Place and Belonging: Ribas documents how people create temporary belonging in marginal spaces — the waste ground as a site of informal community.

title: Yoko Ono: Music of the Mind
author: Tate
tagline: The Tate Modern exhibition catalogue — Ono's instruction pieces, films, music, and activist art.
description: A comprehensive survey of Ono's seven-decade practice, from her early Fluxus instruction pieces through her collaborations with Lennon to her recent peace activism. Art as participation, provocation, and possibility.

title: You Are Here: Art After the Internet
author: Omar Kholeif
tagline: How the internet changed art — post-internet aesthetics, digital culture, and the dissolution of online and offline.
description: This Cornerhouse publication surveys the generation of artists for whom the internet is not a novelty but an ambient condition. Essays and artworks explore how network culture, social media, and digital circulation have transformed the production, distribution, and experience of contemporary art.

themeNotes.The Machine and the Human: Post-internet art responds to a world in which the distinction between human experience and digital mediation has collapsed.
